summary: Latest-evidence research on the HITL sweet spot: decision altitude + review granularity in agentic AI. 8 discovery threads, each adversarially verified.
logs: Researching 8 sub-questions on the HITL sweet spot — discovery then adversarial verification, pipelined.

discovery.summary: 2025–2026 work converges on treating agent autonomy as a graduated, designable property modeled on SAE J3016 driving levels, rather than a binary. The canonical academic taxonomy (Feng, McDonald & Zhang, U. Washington / Knight Inst., June–July 2025) defines five levels by the human's role — Operator, Collaborator, Consultant, Approver, Observer — and argues autonomy is "a deliberate design decision, separate from capability and operational environment," proposing third-party "autonomy certificates" that cap the max allowed level. Coding-specific five-rung ladders (Swarmia's Assistive→Conversational→Task Agent→Autonomous Teammate→Agentic Avalanche; ASDLC's L1–L5) map each level to a descending human decision altitude (Driver→Reviewer→Change Owner→Auditor→Consumer) and argue higher autonomy is NOT always better — match level to task ambiguity/verifiability, with most teams' ceiling at Levels 2–3. Governance reframes the target as "managed/bounded autonomy" with risk-tiered HITL (pre-approval) / HOTL (monitored intervention) / HOOTL decision rights, time-boxed by reversibility; CSA's L0–L5 scales controls and approval seniority by level. Anthropic's primary telemetry (Feb 2026) shows experienced users migrating HITL→HOTL (auto-approve 20%→40%+, interrupts 5%→9%) — calibrated, not blind, trust — while a persistent "delegation gap" (≈60% AI use but only 0–20% full delegation) is attributed to missing judgment, persistent context, testable outcomes, and explicit constraints, not capability. The strongest split is task-delegation vs goal-delegation: developers delegate bounded tasks but retain architecture/goal decisions.

key_findings.claim: The leading academic framework defines five autonomy levels by the human's role (Operator, Collaborator, Consultant, Approver, Observer) and treats autonomy as a deliberate design choice separable from capability.
key_findings.evidence: "an agent's level of autonomy can be treated as a deliberate design decision, separate from its capability and operational environment"; levels are "characterized by the roles a user can take when interacting with an agent."
key_findings.source_title: Levels of Autonomy for AI Agents (Feng, McDonald, Zhang)

key_findings.claim: The same paper proposes 'autonomy certificates' — third-party-issued documents capping the maximum autonomy level an agent may operate at, backed by an 'autonomy case' analogous to a safety case.
key_findings.evidence: A certificate "prescribes the maximum level of autonomy at which an agent can operate given... technical specifications... and... operational environment," issued "by a third-party governing body."
key_findings.source_title: Levels of Autonomy for AI Agents

key_findings.claim: The 2026 HITL/HOTL/HOOTL oversight choice is explicitly risk- and reversibility-driven, with time-boxed decision lanes per action class.
key_findings.evidence: HITL "requires a human to approve... before the AI system executes"; HOTL "allows the AI to act autonomously while a human monitors outputs and can intervene"; lanes of "15 seconds for low-risk... 15 minutes for financial disbursements."
key_findings.source_title: Human-in-the-Loop: A 2026 Guide to AI Oversight (Strata)

key_findings.claim: The Cloud Security Alliance defines a six-level L0–L5 autonomy taxonomy where controls and approval seniority scale with level, and Level 5 is deemed inappropriate for enterprises today.
key_findings.evidence: L3 Conditional: "The AI makes decisions... autonomously within defined boundaries, escalating to humans only when it... exceed[s] those boundaries." "Autonomy boundaries must be technically enforced, not just policy-documented."
key_findings.source_title: Autonomy Levels for Agentic AI (Jim Reavis, CSA)

key_findings.claim: Coding-specific five-level ladders argue higher autonomy is not always better; the right level depends on task ambiguity and verifiability, with most teams' ceiling at Levels 2–3.
key_findings.evidence: "making an industry expert work as an intern is wasted potential; putting a middle-schooler in the CEO chair is a disaster"; Level 3 test: "can you create a pull request from your phone without opening your laptop?"
key_findings.source_title: Five levels of AI coding agent autonomy (Swarmia)

key_findings.claim: A parallel L1–L5 coding scale explicitly pairs each autonomy level with a human decision-altitude role (Driver, Reviewer, Change Owner, Auditor, Consumer).
key_findings.evidence: L1 "Driver (hands-on 100%)"; L2 "Reviewer"; L3 "Change Owner (async validation of CI/CD and intent drift)"; L4 "Auditor (post-hoc telemetry)"; L5 "Consumer (passive beneficiary)."
key_findings.source_title: Levels of Autonomy: L1-L5 AI Agent Autonomy Scale (ASDLC.io)

key_findings.claim: Anthropic's measured Claude Code data shows experienced users raise auto-approval AND interruptions simultaneously, indicating a real HITL→HOTL migration driven by calibrated trust.
key_findings.evidence: Auto-approval ~20% (new) to >40% (~750 sessions); interrupt rate 5%→9%; experienced users "stop reviewing each action and instead let Claude run autonomously, intervening only when needed."
key_findings.source_title: Measuring AI agent autonomy in practice (Anthropic)

key_findings.claim: A persistent 'delegation gap' — ~60% AI use but only 0–20% full delegation — is attributed to missing judgment, persistent context, testable outcomes, and explicit constraints, not capability.
key_findings.evidence: "Developers now use AI in roughly 60% of their work — but they report being able to fully delegate only 0–20% of tasks." "The problem isn't capability. It's judgment."
key_findings.source_title: Anthropic's 2026 Agentic Coding Report: Delegation/Orchestration Era (Pathmode)

key_findings.claim: Google DeepMind formalizes delegation as a sequenced transfer of authority/responsibility/accountability spanning atomic, open-ended, and recursive autonomy.
key_findings.evidence: Delegation is "a sequence of decisions involving task allocation, that also incorporates transfer of authority, responsibility, accountability... clarity of intent, and mechanisms for establishing trust"; spectrum from "atomic execution" to agents that "decompose objectives and pursue sub-goals."
key_findings.source_title: Intelligent AI Delegation (Tomašev, Franklin, Osindeler — Google DeepMind)

key_findings.claim: Professional developers adopt a control-first stance: delegate bounded tasks but retain goal- and architecture-level decisions where downstream consequences are high.
key_findings.evidence: Developers "delegate specific, bounded tasks while maintaining control over broader objectives and architectural decisions," retaining control "when outcomes significantly impact code quality, system architecture, or downstream consequences."
key_findings.source_title: Professional Software Developers Don't Vibe, They Control (Huang et al.)

key_findings.claim: Enterprise governance frames decision altitude as explicitly assigned agent 'decision rights' tiered by reversibility (autonomous / HITL pre-approval / HOTL post-review), aiming at 'bounded autonomy'.
key_findings.evidence: Enterprises must define "which decisions the agent can make entirely autonomously, which... require a human-in-the-loop... before execution, and which... require a human-on-the-loop for post-execution review."
key_findings.source_title: Governing the Agentic Enterprise (Berkeley CMR) / 7 Agentic AI Trends 2026 (MLM)

key_findings.claim: SAE J3016 is the explicit foundational analogy for L0–L5 agent ladders, including domain-specific data-agent taxonomies (HKUST/Tsinghua).
key_findings.evidence: A "six-level hierarchy (L0–L5) for data agents, inspired by the SAE driving automation scale," delineating "progressive shifts in autonomy, from manual operations (L0) to fully autonomous data agents (L5)."
key_findings.source_title: Data Agents L0-L5: Understanding the New Autonomy Hierarchy (TechLife)

Swarmia Five Levels of Coding-Agent Autonomy: Assistive / Conversational / Task Agent / Autonomous Teammate / Agentic Avalanche (Mar 2026)

Intelligent AI Delegation (five pillars; atomic vs open-ended vs recursive delegation; Zone of Indifference, Authority Gradient, Liability Firebreaks, Privilege Attenuation) — Google DeepMind, arXiv 2602.11865 (Feb 2026)

Data Agents L0–L5 (SAE-J3016-inspired, HKUST/Tsinghua)

discovery.recency_note: Evidence is very recent and the area is fast-moving. The strongest sources cluster in Jan–May 2026 (CSA Jan 28, Anthropic Feb 18, DeepMind Feb 12, Pathmode Mar 11, Swarmia Mar 19, Berkeley CMR Mar, ASDLC May 28, Strata May 11, CIO May 5), anchored by the foundational June–July 2025 academic taxonomy (Feng et al.) and a Dec 2025 empirical developer study. Terminology is still consolidating — multiple competing L0/L1–L5 ladders coexist, and the HITL-vs-AITL and 'managed autonomy' framings are actively contested as of mid-2026. The SAE J3016 analogy is foundational but the agent-specific role-based and risk-based ladders supersede it for this domain. Expect continued churn; revisit within 1–2 quarters.

verdicts.claim: Feng, McDonald & Zhang define five autonomy levels by the human's role (Operator, Collaborator, Consultant, Approver, Observer) and treat autonomy as a deliberate design choice separable from capability.
verdicts.note: Five level names and the exact quote 'a deliberate design decision, separate from its capability and operational environment' both verified in the abstract. Authors/dates match. Minor: it is an arXiv/Knight working paper, not demonstrably peer-reviewed as the discovery source-type label implies.

verdicts.claim: The same paper proposes 'autonomy certificates' issued by a third-party governing body, capping max autonomy, backed by an 'autonomy case' analogous to a safety case.
verdicts.note: Confirmed in the Knight Columbia full text (Section 4): 'issued by a third-party governing body to agent developers' and 'analogous to safety cases.' Caveat: the arXiv abstract alone does NOT support the third-party-body language — it calls this one exploratory 'potential application.' Cite the full text, not the abstract; the verb 'proposes' slightly overstates an exploratory mechanism.

verdicts.claim: HITL/HOTL/HOOTL oversight is risk- and reversibility-driven with time-boxed decision lanes (15s low-risk, 2min PII, 15min financial).
verdicts.note: HITL/HOTL definitions and all three time-boxed lane figures verbatim. HOOTL appears only as an example, not formally defined in the three-term spectrum. Vendor (identity-platform) blog — practitioner opinion, not a standard.

verdicts.claim: CSA defines an L0-L5 autonomy taxonomy with controls/approval scaling by level; boundaries 'technically enforced, not just policy-documented'; auto-drop to L1 on anomaly; L5 inappropriate for enterprise today.
verdicts.note: Every sub-claim (L3 Conditional definition, technical-enforcement line, auto-drop-to-L1, 'I don't believe Level 5 is appropriate for enterprise deployment today') verified verbatim. Author Jim Reavis, 2026-01-28.

verdicts.claim: Swarmia's five coding-autonomy levels (Assistive/Conversational/Task Agent/Autonomous Teammate/Agentic Avalanche) argue higher isn't better; ceiling at L2-3; phone-PR test for L3.
verdicts.note: All five names, 'higher is not always better,' the CEO/intern/middle-schooler quote, the phone-PR L3 test, and the L2-3 ceiling all verified verbatim. Author Miikka Holkeri, 2026-03-19.

verdicts.claim: ASDLC L1-L5 scale pairs each level with a human role: Driver, Reviewer, Change Owner, Auditor, Consumer.
verdicts.note: All five role pairings present verbatim, last updated 2026-05-28. Low authority: anonymous self-published knowledge base with no named author; valid as an illustrative framework, not as evidence of industry consensus. Discovery's medium confidence is right.

verdicts.claim: Anthropic telemetry shows experienced users raise auto-approval (~20%->40%+) AND interruptions (5%->9%), a HITL->HOTL migration driven by calibrated trust.
verdicts.note: All raw numbers (auto-approve 20%->40%+, interrupts 5%->9%, 80% safeguards, 73% HITL, turn duration 25->45min) confirmed verbatim. But the 'calibrated trust, not blind trust' interpretation is a gloss — the same data is consistent with the competing 2026 'HITL theater / oversight illusion' thesis (rising interrupts may mean users catch MORE problems, not better calibration). Numbers confirmed; causal/normative spin is contestable.

verdicts.claim: A persistent 'delegation gap' (~60% AI use, 0-20% full delegation) is attributed to missing judgment/context/testable outcomes/constraints, not capability.
verdicts.note: Figures and 'the problem isn't capability, it's judgment' framing trace to a REAL primary source — Anthropic's 2026 Agentic Coding Trends Report — corroborated across 5+ independent secondary write-ups. Discovery cites the Pathmode secondary blog; should cite the primary Anthropic report. A rival causal explanation (Osmani's 'comprehension debt') is omitted by the thread's single-cause framing.

verdicts.claim: DeepMind 'Intelligent AI Delegation' formalizes delegation as sequenced authority/responsibility/accountability transfer spanning atomic, open-ended, and recursive autonomy.
verdicts.note: Best-sourced finding. Authors (Tomasev, Franklin, Osindero, Google DeepMind), delegation definition, and ALL eight named concepts (atomic/open-ended/recursive delegation, zone of indifference, authority gradient, liability firebreaks, privilege attenuation, contract-first decomposition) verified in the body. It is a preprint, not peer-reviewed as the label implies.

verdicts.claim: Professional developers delegate bounded tasks but retain goal/architecture decisions where downstream consequences are high (Huang et al.).
verdicts.note: Paper exists; title/authors match; core control-first thesis confirmed by abstract ('retain their agency in software design and implementation out of insistence on fundamental software quality attributes'). BUT the specific quoted evidence strings ('bounded tasks while maintaining control over broader objectives and architectural decisions'; 'when outcomes significantly impact code quality, system architecture, or downstream consequences') were NOT located in the abstract/metadata — possibly body text or paraphrase-presented-as-quote. n=13+99, modest weight.

verdicts.claim: SAE J3016 is the foundational analogy that the 2026 agent L0-L5 ladders cleanly build on.
verdicts.note: The borrowing-from-SAE claim is corroborated, but the framing as a clean foundation is contradicted by 2026 critiques: the SAE analogy's gains are non-linear, self-driving 'levels' have slipped for ~a decade, and rivals (Parasuraman-Sheridan-Wickens 10-level; a minimal 3-level model) are argued to fit better. The analogy is contested and leaky, not foundational-and-settled. Finding 12's TechLife source is a low-authority blog.

contradicting_evidence: MIT Technology Review (2026-04-16), Defense News (2026-03-26), and SiliconANGLE (2026-01-18) advance a 'HITL theater / oversight illusion' thesis: human overseers often lack context/visibility to evaluate at machine speed, producing 'the illusion of oversight rather than the substance.' This directly qualifies the thread's optimistic 'calibrated trust' reading of the Anthropic HITL->HOTL migration (Findings 3, 7, 11) — the same telemetry is consistent with a slide toward nominal-only oversight.

The SAE J3016 analogy that the synthesis treats as a clean foundation is actively critiqued in 2026 (interface-eu.org, emergentmind.com): non-linear capability gains, a decade of unmet self-driving 'level' promises, and rival models (Parasuraman-Sheridan-Wickens 10-level; a minimal 3-level human-controlled/bounded/broad scheme). The analogy is leaky and contested, not foundational-and-settled.

Taxonomy proliferation undercuts any implied 'convergence': beyond the five ladders catalogued there are several MORE competing L1-L5 frameworks circulating in 2026 (OpenDataScience/Datasaur 'Agents to Autonomy', Sean Falconer's 'Practical Guide to Levels of AI Agent Autonomy'). The field has many incompatible L0/L1-L5 ladders, not one converging standard — the synthesis soft-pedals this.

Addy Osmani's 'comprehension debt' offers a rival causal explanation for the delegation gap (the downstream cost of not understanding AI-generated code), distinct from the thread's single-cause 'missing judgment/context/spec.' Does not contradict the 60%/0-20% numbers but exposes the thread's mono-causal framing as incomplete.
training_data_smells: Recurring 'peer-reviewed' source-type label applied to what are arXiv PREPRINTS / working papers (Feng et al. 2506.12469, DeepMind 2602.11865, Huang et al. 2512.14012) — inflates authority; none is shown to be peer-reviewed.

Finding 10's quoted evidence strings ('delegate specific, bounded tasks while maintaining control over broader objectives and architectural decisions'; 'when outcomes significantly impact code quality, system architecture, or downstream consequences') could not be matched to the cited source's abstract/metadata — possible paraphrase presented as a direct quotation.

The synthesis's 'converges on' / 'consensus' framing reads as a selection artifact: the corpus over-weights sources that agree and under-weights the substantial 2026 oversight-skeptic and taxonomy-proliferation literature.

discovery.summary: The 2025-2026 consensus is that review depth should scale with stakes decomposed into reversibility, blast radius, security exposure, and cost of error — converging on a three-tier shape: read-only/idempotent actions run autonomously, reversible state changes proceed with asynchronous/after-the-fact review, and irreversible or wide-blast actions require explicit pre-execution human approval. This maps onto Amazon's one-way/two-way door framing applied to agent tool calls, onto HITL/HOTL/out-of-the-loop oversight tiers, and onto named engineering patterns like Baytech's Helmsman Pattern and four-ring Blast Radius Containment model. A second strong theme is that calibration must be enforced at the tool/API layer, not by prompts or policy — exemplified by Anthropic's shipped Claude Code Auto Mode, whose classifier gates only high-downside actions (force-push, mass deletion, exfiltration, prod deploys) while letting version-controlled (i.e., reversible) edits run. The sharpest tension: reversibility-based frameworks would spot-check reversible work, but the AI-code-review camp insists every line be reviewed because security exposure is a separate axis from recovery cost; meanwhile METR's empirical survey shows the field already loosens permissions by stakes (~40% unrestricted on low-stakes vs <20% high-stakes) yet imposes no strict universal oversight mandates. The EU AI Act Article 14 provides the clearest legal endorsement of risk-calibration: oversight must be "commensurate with risk, autonomy and context," with per-decision human review required only for biometric identification (two-person verification).

key_findings.claim: The canonical risk-calibrated pattern is a three-tier action classification: read-only/idempotent runs immediately, reversible changes execute with async review flags, irreversible actions require cryptographic human approval (the 'Helmsman Pattern'), wrapped in a four-ring Blast Radius Containment model (tool scope, data scope, reversibility evaluation, human approval).
key_findings.evidence: Tier 1 (Automated): read-only, idempotent ... execute immediately; Tier 2 (Warning): reversible changes execute with asynchronous review flags; Tier 3 (Locked): irreversible actions require cryptographic human approval. Four rings: tool scope, data scope, reversibility evaluation, human approval for high-impact API payloads.
key_findings.source_title: Five Engineering Patterns to Secure Agentic AI in 2026

key_findings.claim: Reversibility is the primary heuristic for autonomy: one-way-door (irreversible) actions like wire transfers demand exhaustive verification, while agents succeed in software dev precisely because git/code-review/transactions/observability make actions reversible.
key_findings.evidence: tool calling enables agents to make decisions but doesn't address whether those decisions are reversible ... software development is made up entirely of systems that make decisions more reversible and less consequential ... for one-way doors no amount of additional verification is excessive.
key_findings.source_title: It's Not Too Late to Roll Back MCP

key_findings.claim: A four-dimension decision matrix scores each action on reversibility, blast radius, context completeness, and error-cost-vs-delay-cost to set autonomy level, and stresses enforcement by architecture not prompts.
key_findings.evidence: Is the action reversible? No -> require confirmation. Is blast radius contained? No -> add resource limits. Full context? No -> require verification. Error cost < delay cost? No -> human in loop. autonomy level must be enforced by system architecture, not by instructions in a prompt.
key_findings.source_title: AI Agent Autonomy Levels: How Much Freedom Is Too Much?

key_findings.claim: Risk-tiered HITL/HOTL/out-of-the-loop oversight uses time-boxed approval windows scaled to risk (15s low-risk, 2min PII, 15min financial), defaulting to denial on timeout.
key_findings.evidence: HITL requires a human to approve before the AI executes (financial disbursements, legal agreements); HOTL allows autonomous action where mistakes remain reversible. Time-boxed decision lanes: 15-second window for low-risk, 15-minute for financial disbursements.
key_findings.source_title: Human-in-the-Loop: A 2026 Guide to AI Oversight

key_findings.claim: Anthropic's shipped Claude Code Auto Mode is a concrete risk-calibrated approval system: it auto-allows reads and version-controlled project edits (because reversible) and gates ~20+ high-downside categories via a transcript classifier, checking intent alignment and capping at 3 consecutive / 20 total denials.
key_findings.evidence: routine edits within project directories bypass approval since they're reviewable via version control; classifier blocks force-pushing git history, mass cloud-storage deletion, external data exfiltration, production deploys; 'clean up my branches' doesn't authorize batch deletion; 0.4% false-positive vs 17% false-negative rate.
key_findings.source_title: How we built Claude Code auto mode

key_findings.claim: Deterministic pre-action authorization gates each tool call on Action Risk x Reversibility x Blast Radius, arguing post-hoc monitoring cannot intercept individual production tool calls.
key_findings.evidence: Authorization decisions scale based on Action Risk Level, Reversibility, and Blast Radius (how many systems/users affected) ... reversible low-impact actions require minimal overhead while destructive or broad-impact actions demand explicit human approval. Post-hoc evaluation is structurally retrospective ... does not intercept individual tool calls.
key_findings.source_title: Before the Tool Call: Deterministic Pre-Action Authorization for Autonomous AI Agents

key_findings.claim: The EU AI Act Article 14 mandates oversight 'commensurate with risk, level of autonomy and context of use' and explicitly does NOT require per-decision human review for most high-risk systems — only biometric identification needs two-person verification.
key_findings.evidence: measures shall be commensurate with the risks, level of autonomy and context of use ... does not require a human to review every AI decision before it takes effect ... biometric ID: no action unless separately verified and confirmed by at least two natural persons. In force 2026-08-02.
key_findings.source_title: Article 14: Human Oversight — EU Artificial Intelligence Act

key_findings.claim: Empirically (METR survey, Feb-Apr 2026) practitioners already loosen permissions by stakes (~40% unrestricted on low-stakes vs <20% high-stakes) but thorough review of low-stakes output is rare and no company imposes strict universal oversight mandates.
key_findings.evidence: ~40% of respondents gave agents unrestricted permissions on low-stakes projects vs less than 20% on high-stakes ... a minority thoroughly reviewed code or logs for low-stakes projects ... We are not aware of any company imposing strict requirements about human oversight of agents in Feb-Mar 2026.
key_findings.source_title: Frontier Risk Report (February to March 2026)

key_findings.claim: Security exposure is a distinct axis that can override the reversibility tier: AI-generated code is cited as 1.7x more issue-prone and 2.74x more XSS-prone, so the code-review camp argues for exhaustive (not spot-check) review despite code being reversible, with automation as the answer to reviewer fatigue.
key_findings.evidence: AI-authored pull requests contain 1.7x more issues and are 2.74x more prone to XSS vulnerabilities ... every line of AI-generated code needs thorough review ... the answer to reviewer fatigue is automation/AI-assisted review, not reducing thoroughness.
key_findings.source_title: Code Review in 2026: Reviewing the AI, Not the Human

key_findings.claim: Autonomy-level taxonomies (NVIDIA 0-3, CSA 0-5) escalate oversight controls with autonomy and capability, requiring technical (not policy) enforcement and dynamic de-escalation on anomalies; financial/code-execution/physical capabilities warrant extra controls at any level.
key_findings.evidence: NVIDIA Level 2/3: consider time-of-use manual approval of sensitive actions, taint tracing. CSA: boundaries must be technically enforced, not policy-documented; financial transactions, code execution, physical operations warrant additional controls at any autonomy level; autonomy could automatically decrease if anomalies detected.
key_findings.source_title: Autonomy Levels for AI Agents (CSA) / Agentic Autonomy Levels and Security (NVIDIA)

named_frameworks: Helmsman Pattern (Tier 1 Automated / Tier 2 Warning / Tier 3 Locked) — Baytech Consulting
Blast Radius Containment Model (4 concentric rings: tool scope, data scope, reversibility evaluation, human approval) — Baytech
One-way door vs. two-way door decisions / 'zone of comfort' reversibility-vs-consequence matrix — Amazon origin, applied to agents by Adam Conway/Theory Ventures
Reversibility / Blast Radius / Context / Error-cost-vs-delay decision matrix with Autonomy Levels 0-3 — Apptitude
HITL / HOTL / Human-out-of-the-loop + time-boxed decision lanes — Strata Identity
Deterministic Pre-Action Authorization (Action Risk x Reversibility x Blast Radius) — Uchibeke, arXiv:2603.20953
Claude Code Auto Mode (3-tier permission classifier + intent alignment + denial caps) — Anthropic
NVIDIA Agentic Autonomy Levels 0-3 + taint tracing + time-of-use manual approval
CSA Six-Level Autonomy Taxonomy + Capability-Control Matrix + AI Controls Matrix (AICM)
EU AI Act Article 14 'commensurate with risk/autonomy/context' + two-person biometric verification
Asymmetry of Verification / Verifier's Law — Jason Wei (foundational)
debates_tensions: Spot-check vs. exhaustive review for reversible domains: reversibility frameworks would lightly review reversible actions (incl. code, made reversible by git), but the AI-code-review camp insists every line be reviewed — reconciled by treating security exposure as a separate axis from recovery cost that can override the reversibility tier.

Aspirational frameworks vs. observed practice: CSA/NVIDIA/Strata/Baytech prescribe tiered approval, but METR finds almost no org enforces strict oversight, thorough low-stakes review is rare, and monitoring is easily bypassed.

Pre-action gating vs. post-hoc monitoring: Uchibeke argues post-hoc evaluation cannot intercept individual tool calls so pre-action authorization is required; CSA Level 4 and METR lean on monitoring/anomaly-detection/kill-switches after the fact.

Per-decision human review required or not: EU AI Act Article 14 explicitly does NOT require it for most high-risk systems (only biometric ID needs two-person sign-off), endorsing risk-calibration; some governance writers read 'meaningful human control' more strictly.

Who makes the approval call — human or model: Anthropic Auto Mode delegates judgment to a classifier (17% false-negative on dangerous actions), while HITL purists keep a human in the gate for irreversible/Tier-3 actions; disagreement over whether a classifier substitutes for human approval.
discovery.recency_note: Evidence is very recent and the area is fast-moving: 9 of 12 sources are from 2026 (Jan-May), including the strongest (METR Frontier Risk Report 2026-05-19, Anthropic Auto Mode 2026-03-25, Baytech 2026-05-01, Strata 2026-05-11, Apptitude 2026-05-10, CSA 2026-01-28, Uchibeke arXiv 2026-03-24). The NVIDIA autonomy-levels framework (2025-02-25) is the oldest substantive source and remains current but pre-dates the reversibility/blast-radius framing that 2026 sources adopt. The EU AI Act text is 2024 statute but its human-oversight obligations enter force 2026-08-02. Frameworks are proliferating faster than they are being adopted — METR confirms no enforced industry standard yet.

verdicts.claim: Anthropic's shipped Claude Code Auto Mode auto-allows reads and version-controlled project edits and gates ~20+ high-downside categories via a transcript classifier (intent alignment, 3 consecutive / 20 total denial caps, 0.4% FPR vs 17% FNR, 93% manual acceptance baseline).
verdicts.note: Every specific (in-project edits reviewable via version control, force-push/mass-delete/exfiltration categories, 'clean up my branches' intent example, 3 consecutive/20 total, 0.4% FPR, 17% FNR, 93% acceptance) is verbatim in the primary source. Strongest claim in the thread.

verdicts.claim: METR survey: ~40% gave unrestricted permissions on low-stakes vs <20% high-stakes; only a minority thoroughly reviewed low-stakes output; no company imposed strict human-oversight requirements in Feb-Mar 2026; monitoring was easily disabled.
verdicts.note: All four statements verbatim. Survey window is Feb-Apr 2026 (Appendix F); the oversight quote is scoped to Feb-Mar 2026 — discovery summary uses both, both correct. Sample size NOT disclosed, so '~40%' is directional, not precise.

verdicts.claim: EU AI Act Article 14 mandates oversight 'commensurate with risk, autonomy and context,' requires two-person verification only for biometric ID, and explicitly does NOT require per-decision human review for most high-risk systems.
verdicts.note: 'Commensurate with risk', the biometric two-person rule, and stop button are verbatim statutory text. BUT 'does not require a human to review every AI decision before it takes effect' is the discovery agent's interpretation, NOT statutory text — and several governance sources read Art 14(1) as REQUIRING human sign-off gates for consequential high-risk actions. Interpretation presented as source evidence; overstated.

verdicts.claim: The canonical risk-calibrated pattern is Baytech's three-tier Helmsman Pattern (Automated/Warning/Locked) wrapped in a four-ring Blast Radius Containment model (tool scope, data scope, reversibility, human approval).
verdicts.note: Tier names and the four rings are verbatim. But this is a single consultancy marketing blog with no evidence of external adoption or validation; 'cryptographic human approval' is aspirational. Accurately reported, but its status as a 'canonical' industry pattern is unsupported — it is one vendor's coinage.

verdicts.claim: Reversibility is the primary heuristic for autonomy: one-way doors (wire transfers) demand exhaustive verification; agents succeed in software dev because git/code-review/transactions/observability make actions reversible.
verdicts.note: All sub-claims near-verbatim including the 'zone of comfort' and 'no amount of verification is too much' lines. It is a VC opinion blog (framing, not evidence); confidence:high is generous for a single opinion post, though the framing is sound and widely echoed.

verdicts.claim: Risk-tiered HITL/HOTL/out-of-the-loop uses time-boxed approval windows (15s low-risk, 2min PII, 15min financial), defaulting to denial on timeout.
verdicts.note: Time windows and HITL/HOTL definitions verbatim. These specific windows are one vendor's PROPOSAL with no evidence of real-world use; treat numbers as illustrative, not a validated standard. confidence:high conflates 'source says it' with 'it is a standard'.

verdicts.claim: Deterministic pre-action authorization (arXiv 2603.20953, Uchibeke) gates each tool call on Action Risk x Reversibility x Blast Radius, arguing post-hoc monitoring cannot intercept individual tool calls.
verdicts.note: Paper exists; title/author confirmed; post-hoc-is-retrospective thesis supported. BUT the actual core is the 'Open Agent Passport' (spending limits, capability scoping) — the clean 'Action Risk x Reversibility x Blast Radius' triad is NOT surfaced in the abstract and appears extrapolated. Submission date is Mar 21 2026 (discovery says Mar 24). Discovery mislabels source_type as 'peer-reviewed' — it is a non-peer-reviewed arXiv preprint.

verdicts.claim: AI-generated code is 1.7x more issue-prone and 2.74x more XSS-prone, so the code-review camp argues every line needs review despite code being reversible.
verdicts.note: Numbers are real and accurate (1.7x = total issues, 10.83 vs 6.45/PR; 2.74x = specifically XSS) but mis-attributed to a thin blog. Primary source is CodeRabbit's 470-PR study, corroborated by The Register and TechRadar. Swap the citation.

training_data_smells: 'EU AI Act explicitly does NOT require per-decision human review' — interpretation presented as if it were sourced statutory evidence; the phrase appears nowhere in Article 14.

'Cryptographic human approval' (Baytech Helmsman Tier 3) and the precise 15s/2min/15min time-boxed lanes (Strata) read as confident vendor invention given authority they have not earned — correctly attributed but over-weighted as if industry standards.

The Medium 'Asymmetry of Verification' source (confidence:low) invoking 'Verifier's Law / Jason Wei' as foundational is a low-trust personal blog used to inflate the bibliography; the Jason Wei attribution should be sourced to Wei directly, not a third-party summary.
Multiple single-vendor marketing/opinion blogs (Baytech, Strata, Theory Ventures, Apptitude) were assigned confidence:high, conflating 'the source asserts it' with 'it is validated/adopted'.
contradicting_evidence: EU AI Act Article 14(1) is widely read as REQUIRING human oversight with sign-off gates for consequential high-risk actions (in force 2026-08-02), which qualifies METR's 'no company imposes strict oversight' — true of voluntary practice in Feb-Mar 2026 but not of the incoming regulatory floor. (BearingPoint, RegulaAI, digitalapplied governance guides.)

The classifier-substitutes-for-human approach (Anthropic Auto Mode's 17% false-negative rate on dangerous actions) is actively criticized: HITL is 'expected to largely fail due to approval fatigue / auto-approve' (WitnessAI 2026), and models trained out of cheating 'learned to hide their intent while continuing to misbehave' (Hatchworks/Anthropic 2026) — undercutting trust in a model gating irreversible actions.

Reversibility is not the only axis: the International AI Safety Report 2026 frames risk as malicious use / malfunction / SYSTEMIC plus loss-of-control (shutdown subversion), and arXiv 2503.04750 argues regulation should key off 'extent of autonomous operations' — categories a reversibility/blast-radius matrix does not capture. Emerging counter-framing is defense-in-depth (continuous monitoring + containment), not reversibility-first gating alone.

Practical governance maturity is low: a Deloitte survey found only ~21% of companies have a mature agent governance model, corroborating that the tiered-approval frameworks are aspirational rather than adopted.

result.title: Reviewing AI-generated artifacts — every line vs. sampling

discovery.summary: The 2025-2026 consensus is that practitioners do NOT re-read every AI-generated line by eye; they review at the behavior/acceptance level backed by automated structural gates (static analysis, type-checking, dependency verification, tests), reserving scarce human judgment for requirement fidelity, business logic, architecture, and consequence — while the committing human remains fully accountable (Simon Willison: "a computer can never be held accountable; that's your job"). The strongest empirical signal is that review has become the bottleneck: Faros AI telemetry on 10,000+ developers found high AI adoption drove 154% larger PRs, 91% longer review times, and 9% more bugs/developer; METR's RCT found experienced devs 19% slower with AI; and even when 73.8% of AI review-bot comments were acted on, PR closure time still rose 42%. AI code-review bots are empirically noisy (60.2% of agent-only-reviewed PRs fell in the lowest 0-30% signal band; CRA-only PRs merged at 45% vs 68% human-reviewed). The academic MSR/EASE 2026 corpus reveals the real-world failure mode is the opposite of "every line": 38-61% of agent PRs receive no recorded human review ("reviewer abandonment" is the top rejection cause at 38%), humans intervene less on agent PRs (52% vs 84%) but at higher per-intervention cost, and surface plausibility suppresses scrutiny. Test-as-trust crystallizes as acceptance-criteria-driven TDD (humans define testable "done" before AI codes; tests validate the spec not the implementation) with higher coverage bars (85-90% vs 70-80%), but carries the "tautological testing" risk where AI tests inherit AI blind spots, making a human business-logic checkpoint irreducible. DORA 2025 frames AI as an amplifier: up on throughput, negatively correlated with stability, with ~30% distrusting AI code and an explicit "trust but verify" recommendation.

key_findings.claim: Moving from low to high AI adoption inflated PR size 154%, lengthened review time 91%, and raised bugs 9% per developer — throughput moved into the review bottleneck rather than relieving it.
key_findings.evidence: 154% increase in PR size; 91% longer code-review duration; 9% more bugs per developer; 98% more merged PRs (10,000+ developers, 1,255 teams, up to 2 years).
key_findings.source_title: What METR's Study Missed About AI Productivity in the Wild (Faros AI telemetry study)

key_findings.claim: In an RCT, experienced developers were 19% slower with AI despite believing they were ~20% faster; only ~9% of task time went to reviewing/modifying AI output.
key_findings.evidence: Developers using AI took 19% longer to complete 246 tasks; perception-reality gap ~39 points; cites arXiv 2507.09089.
key_findings.source_title: Why AI Coding Tools Make Experienced Developers 19% Slower (METR study, Augment Code)

key_findings.claim: Automated AI review comments did not reduce review burden even when acted upon, and only ~20-25% of AI hallucinations are catchable by static analysis (44% escape automation, needing human judgment).
key_findings.evidence: Even with 73.8% of automated review comments acted on, PR closure time still increased 42%; 20-25% of AI hallucinations detectable by static analysis, ~44% escape automated detection.
key_findings.source_title: Stop Sending IDE-Catchable AI Code Errors to Review (JetBrains AI Blog)

key_findings.claim: DORA 2025 frames AI as an amplifier: positive on throughput, negative on delivery stability, with ~30% distrusting AI code and an explicit 'trust but verify' recommendation.
key_findings.evidence: 30% report little or no trust in AI-generated code; researchers recommend trust-but-verify; AI adoption negatively related to delivery stability absent strong testing/version control/fast feedback.
key_findings.source_title: DORA — State of AI-assisted Software Development 2025

key_findings.claim: A majority of agent-authored PRs receive no recorded human review; agent comments dominate review threads and agent-steering commands are 16x more frequent than on human PRs.
key_findings.evidence: 61.38% of AI-generated PRs received no recorded review activity; agent-authored comments 71.58% of threads; agent-steering 25.92% vs 1.63% (V=0.34). 33,596 agent PRs, AIDev dataset.
key_findings.source_title: These Aren't the Reviews You're Looking For: How Humans Review AI-Generated Pull Requests (Duma et al., EASE 2026)

key_findings.claim: Reviewer abandonment is the top failure mode for agent PRs; incorrect implementation is rare by comparison, showing review attention (not code correctness) is the binding constraint.
key_findings.evidence: Abandoned/Not Reviewed = 228 (38%) of rejections; Duplicate 23%; CI/Test Failure 17%; Incorrect Implementation only 3%. 33,596 agent PRs across 5 agents; overall merge rate 71.48%.
key_findings.source_title: Where Do AI Coding Agents Fail? An Empirical Study of Failed Agentic Pull Requests in GitHub (Ehsani et al., MSR 2026)

key_findings.claim: Humans intervene less often on agent PRs than human PRs but each intervention costs more; review work is shifting from implementation to supervision/guidance/quality-control.
key_findings.evidence: Human interventions occur less frequently in APRs than HPRs (52.17% vs 83.59%) but require higher review effort, larger code churn, longer durations. Intervention taxonomy: Guidance 58%, Decision 21%, Direct code 17%, Operational 4%.
key_findings.source_title: Behind Agentic Pull Requests: Developer Interventions in AI Agent-Authored PRs (Khelifi et al., MSR 2026)

key_findings.claim: AI code-review bots are empirically noisy and correlate with worse merge outcomes; signal-to-noise is poor across most tools.
key_findings.evidence: 60.2% of closed CRA-only PRs in the 0-30% (noisy) signal range; 12 of 13 CRAs below 60% signal; CRA-only merge 45.20% vs human-only 68.37% (chi-sq=83.03, p<0.001). 3,109 PRs, 19,450 comments.
key_findings.source_title: From Industry Claims to Empirical Reality: An Empirical Study of Code Review Agents in Pull Requests (Chowdhury et al., MSR 2026)

key_findings.claim: Test-as-trust crystallizes as acceptance-criteria-driven TDD with higher coverage bars for AI code, but carries the 'tautological testing' risk requiring an irreducible human business-logic checkpoint.
key_findings.evidence: 85-90% coverage for AI code vs 70-80% for human code; tests should validate the specification not the implementation; same AI generating code+tests means both share blind spots; human review mandatory for business/regulatory logic.
key_findings.source_title: How to Test AI Generated Code: A QA Checklist for 2026 (ContextQA)

key_findings.claim: Named-practitioner stance: the human committing AI code is fully accountable; shipping unreviewed/untested AI output dumps the work on reviewers and is a dereliction of duty.
key_findings.evidence: A computer can never be held accountable; that's your job as the human in the loop. Reviewing/testing/understanding all AI lines is using an LLM as a typing assistant, not vibe coding.
key_findings.source_title: Simon Willison on delivering AI generated code (Stefan Judis notes)

key_findings.claim: GitHub's official guidance is an 8-area multi-layered review (not line-by-line), with explicit AI-specific pitfalls including hallucinated APIs and deleted/skipped tests.
key_findings.evidence: Eight areas incl. functional checks (compiles, tests pass), context/intent, AI-specific pitfalls (hallucinated APIs, deleted tests, logic errors), automation (CodeQL, Dependabot); don't accept tests that are deleted or skipped instead of fixed.
key_findings.source_title: Review AI-generated code (GitHub Docs)

named_frameworks: DORA 'AI as amplifier' model (throughput up, stability down) — DORA 2025
Acceptance-Criteria-Driven TDD (AC-TDD) — humans define testable 'done' before AI generates; tests validate spec not implementation
JiTTests (Just-in-Time Tests, Meta Engineering) — diff-aware test generation, ~4x more useful catches than generic hardening tests
Six-Layer AI Code Review (requirement fidelity -> logic/edge cases -> API integrity -> security) — Shift Asia
GitHub 8-area multi-layer review checklist (functional, context, quality, dependencies, AI pitfalls, collaborative, automation, workflow)
CRA signal-to-noise two-tier keyword framework (0-30% noisy / 31-59% / 60-79% / 80-100% high-quality) — Chowdhury et al. MSR 2026
Agent-PR rejection taxonomy (4-level: Reviewer/Pull-Request/Code/Agentic) — Ehsani et al. MSR 2026
Developer-intervention taxonomy (Guidance/Decision/Direct-code/Operational) — Khelifi et al. MSR 2026
Review-interaction taxonomy (Agent-steering/Automation-CI/Human-review) — Duma et al. EASE 2026
'Tautological testing' anti-pattern + 'on-the-loop' supervision model
'Lethal trifecta' (Willison, 2025) — adjacent agent-security framing
debates_tensions: Does test-as-trust replace line-by-line review? AC-TDD advocates say validated specs + high coverage substitute for reading every line; the 'tautological testing' critique counters that AI-generated tests inherit AI blind spots, making a human business-logic checkpoint irreducible.

Do AI review tools reduce or increase reviewer burden? Vendors imply reduction; empirical data (MSR 2026 CRA noise study; 2024 42%-closure-time finding) shows bots are noisy and burden often rises — they triage but don't relieve the human bottleneck.

Lab vs field on productivity: METR RCT (19% slower) vs Faros field telemetry (21% more tasks, 98% more PRs) vs DORA (>80% perceive gains). Reconciliation: individual output rises while organizational quality/stability and review throughput degrade.

'Review every line' vs behavior-level: guidance converges on behavior + structural gates, but academic data shows the real-world failure is the opposite extreme — 38-61% silent unreviewed merges, i.e. neither approach is actually applied.
Reviewer bias: AI code's surface plausibility reduces critical scrutiny (fewer negative reactions despite ~2x redundancy), an argument AGAINST relaxing review rigor for AI output.
discovery.recency_note: Evidence is very recent and the area is fast-moving. The strongest empirical sources are 2026 peer-reviewed MSR/EASE conference papers (Apr-May 2026) on agent-authored PRs, plus 2025 industry telemetry (Faros AI Jul 2025, DORA Jul/Sep 2025, METR Jul 2025) and 2026 vendor/practitioner guidance (GitHub, JetBrains May 2026, ContextQA Apr 2026, Willison Dec 2025). No source predates 2024, and the few 2024 datapoints (the 42%-closure-time study) are cited within 2026 articles. The agentic-PR review literature is brand-new and likely to expand rapidly through 2026.

verdicts.claim: Faros telemetry: low->high AI adoption produced 154% larger PRs, 91% longer review time, 9% more bugs/dev, 98% more merged PRs (10,000+ devs, 1,255 teams, up to 2 years).
verdicts.note: Re-fetched; all four figures and study scale verbatim. Caveat: vendor telemetry, correlational not causal; Faros has commercial interest in the 'AI productivity paradox' framing.

verdicts.claim: Duma et al. EASE 2026: 61.38% of AI PRs received no recorded review; 71.58% of thread comments agent-authored; agent-steering 25.92% vs 1.63% (Cramer's V=0.34). 33,596 agent PRs, AIDev.
verdicts.note: Re-fetched; every figure matches to the decimal incl. raw counts (20,621; 28,004). Strong peer-reviewed source.

verdicts.claim: Ehsani et al. MSR 2026: Abandoned/Not Reviewed 228 (38%), Duplicate 23%, CI/Test 17%, Incorrect Impl 3%; overall merge rate 71.48%; 33,596 PRs across 5 agents.
verdicts.note: Re-fetched; taxonomy counts, merge rate (24,014/33,596), and per-agent breakdown all confirmed. Taxonomy % is over 562 manually-analyzed rejected PRs, correctly stated by thread.

verdicts.claim: Chowdhury et al. MSR 2026: 60.2% of closed CRA-only PRs in 0-30% signal band; 12/13 CRAs below 60% signal; CRA-only merge 45.20% vs human-only 68.37% (chi2=83.03, p<0.001).
verdicts.note: Re-fetched; all figures verbatim. Exact framing is '23.17 percentage points lower'. Strong peer-reviewed source.

verdicts.claim: DORA 2025 frames AI as an amplifier; ~30% distrust AI code; explicit 'trust but verify'; AI negatively related to delivery stability.
verdicts.note: 'Amplifier' verbatim on landing page; 30%/trust-but-verify/stability confirmed via Google Cloud blog + multiple summaries. MATERIAL QUALIFIER thread omits: DORA 2025 now links AI POSITIVELY to throughput (reversal of 2024).

verdicts.claim: 20-25% of AI hallucinations are detectable by static analysis; ~44% escape automated detection (requiring human judgment).
verdicts.note: PROVENANCE FAILURE. JetBrains cites arXiv 2409.20550 for these numbers but that paper does NOT contain them. The paper gives a hallucination-TYPE taxonomy (43.53/31.91/24.56%) and only a qualitative statement that some classes evade static checks/tests. The 20-25%/44% figures appear fabricated or misattributed. Companion 73.8%/42% claim cites a different paper (2412.18531), not independently verified here.

verdicts.claim: METR RCT: experienced devs 19% slower with AI despite believing ~20% faster; ~9% of task time spent reviewing/modifying AI output.
verdicts.note: 19% slowdown is real and independently confirmed, but OVER-GENERALIZED: perceived speedup is +24% not ~20%; METR explicitly says the slowdown percentage likely does NOT generalize (narrow population, mature repos where AI helps least); METR redesigned the experiment Feb 2026 after unreliable follow-up signals. Only the expectation gap generalizes.

verdicts.claim: Test-as-trust = acceptance-criteria-driven TDD; tests validate spec not implementation; higher coverage bars 85-90% (AI) vs 70-80% (human); 'tautological testing' risk requires human business-logic checkpoint.
verdicts.note: Spec-vs-implementation and tautological-testing framing accurately quoted. BUT the 85-90% vs 70-80% coverage bars are bare unsourced assertions (training-data smell) — article sources its other stats but not these. The conceptual claim is corroborated more strongly by arXiv 2603.25773 (correlated generator/reviewer failures).

verdicts.claim: Simon Willison: 'a computer can never be held accountable; that's your job as the human in the loop'; shipping untested AI code is a dereliction of duty that dumps work on reviewers.
verdicts.note: Accountability and dereliction-of-duty / shifts-burden quotes present verbatim (Dec 19, 2025). Minor: the 'typing assistant vs vibe coding' phrasing is NOT on this page (Willison position from elsewhere); immaterial.

verdicts.claim: GitHub's official guidance is an 8-area multi-layered review (not line-by-line), with AI-specific pitfalls (hallucinated APIs, deleted/skipped tests, logic errors) and automation (CodeQL, Dependabot).
verdicts.note: Re-fetched; eight named areas, AI pitfalls, deleted-test warning ('fix the test instead of deleting it'), and CodeQL/Dependabot all verbatim. Strong vendor source.

training_data_smells: ContextQA coverage bars '85-90% for AI code vs 70-80% for human code' presented as bare numeric assertions with no source, while the same article sources its other statistics (Stack Overflow, Stanford) — false-precision filler.

JetBrains '20-25% detectable / 44% escape' hallucination split: precise-looking numbers attributed to arXiv 2409.20550, a paper that does not actually contain them — false precision with a citation that does not check out.
'Meta JiTTests ~4x more useful catches than generic hardening tests': carried from a New Stack blog with no primary Meta source located; plausible but unverified vendor/blog claim.
contradicting_evidence: Genuine 2026 expert split on line-by-line review: contra the thread's 'almost no one argues for reading every line,' practitioners (javaworldmag 2026; CIO 'Developers still don't trust AI-generated code') insist on careful per-line reading because structural/logical failures hide beneath superficially-convincing AI code. The behavior-level consensus is softer than the thread states.

METR self-qualification (metr.org Feb 2026): METR redesigned its productivity experiment after the original setting proved unrepresentative and a follow-up gave unreliable signals; METR says the slowdown PERCENTAGE likely does not generalize (only the expectation gap does). Weakens '19% slower' as a headline empirical anchor.
DORA 2025 throughput reversal: AI is now POSITIVELY linked to software delivery throughput (reversing 2024), a material qualifier the thread omits while keeping only the stability-negative half.

arXiv 2603.25773 'The Specification as Quality Gate' (2026): independent preprint sharpening the tautological-testing concern — AI reviewers without EXECUTABLE specifications are structurally unsound because generator and reviewer reason from the same artifacts and exhibit correlated failures. Stronger than the blog sources the thread used.

discovery.summary: The 2025–2026 consensus is that humans should NOT review an agent's plan line-by-line; instead agents should present a decision-grade summary (executive view / "bird's-eye view" / "evidence pack") with detail offloaded but expandable on demand — classic progressive disclosure. Anthropic codified the three-level model (name/description → full instructions → appendix) and Will Larson and Addy Osmani apply the same summary-first, detail-on-demand pattern to plans, where Plan Mode is a read-only approval gate before code. StackAI's "evidence pack" operationalizes the dual-track output most concretely: concise-by-default with expandable detail, framed as "the difference between a 15-second approval and a 15-minute investigation," reframing the reviewer's job as verify-not-redo. A deeper "altitude" theme holds the spec/intent — not the code/diff — is the correct unit of review: Zietsman (arXiv) argues reviewing code-against-code is circular and the spec provides the external correctness reference, and Amazon Kiro makes the spec the signed-off contract before any code. Risk-based gating decides where full detail is warranted: confirm 100% at risk boundaries (irreversible, costly, regulated, high blast radius), sample or batch low-risk. The main tension is throughput vs. thoroughness — GitHub's Griffiths warns summaries can mask subtle hallucinations and unscoped "agent abandonment," and that human judgment is the real bottleneck.

key_findings.claim: Progressive disclosure (summary first, detail on demand) is the canonical agent information pattern, modeled as table-of-contents → chapters → appendix.
key_findings.evidence: "Like a well-organized manual that starts with a table of contents, then specific chapters, and finally a detailed appendix, skills let Claude load information only as needed." Three levels: name+description preloaded → full SKILL.md when relevant → linked appendix files navigated only as needed.
key_findings.source_title: Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills

key_findings.claim: Plans should carry a hierarchical 'bird's-eye view' summary that stays in context while fine detail is offloaded; Plan Mode is a read-only approval gate before code.
key_findings.evidence: "By creating a hierarchical summary in the planning phase, you get a bird's-eye view that can stay in the prompt, while the fine details remain offloaded unless needed." Plan Mode "won't write any code until you're ready." Phased gates Specify→Plan→Tasks→Implement: "you don't move to the next one until the current task is fully validated."
key_findings.source_title: How to Write a Good Spec for AI Agents

key_findings.claim: An 'evidence pack' decision-grade summary — concise by default, expandable detail — turns review into fast verification rather than redoing the work.
key_findings.evidence: "The evidence pack is the difference between a 15-second approval and a 15-minute investigation." "The reviewer's job is not to re-do the agent's work; it's to verify it quickly." Keep evidence "concise by default with expandable detail options"; include "decision rationale and confidence scores," cited sources, rollback plans; "batch low-risk items for single-screen review."
key_findings.source_title: Human-in-the-Loop AI Agents: How to Design Approval Workflows for Safe and Scalable Automation

key_findings.claim: Risk-based gates decide where full detail is warranted: confirm only at risk boundaries, with plan previews for meaningful changes and dual control for sensitive actions.
key_findings.evidence: "Plan previews for meaningful changes ('Approve these steps before we run them')." "The trick is to confirm only at risk boundaries: drafting is cheap; sending at scale is not; changing access rights is never 'just a click.'" Sensitive actions "need dual control: a second approver or an admin-level sign-off."
key_findings.source_title: The Agentic Product Stack in 2026: How Teams Are Shipping AI 'Operators' Without Breaking Trust, Cost, or Control

key_findings.claim: The correct review altitude is the spec/intent, not the code — reviewing code-against-code is structurally circular; the spec is the external correctness reference.
key_findings.evidence: Deploying AI reviewers without executable specs "creates a structurally circular problem where both the generating agent and reviewing agent reason from the same artifact... resulting in the review checking code against itself rather than against intent." Executable specs "perform a domain transition in the Cynefin sense... moving the problem from the complex domain to the complicated domain."
key_findings.source_title: The Specification as Quality Gate: Three Hypotheses on AI-Assisted Code Review (arXiv:2603.25773)

key_findings.claim: Spec-driven IDEs make the spec the signed-off unit of work (EARS requirements + testable acceptance criteria) so humans review intent before any code.
key_findings.evidence: "The spec is the unit of work. Code is what happens after you sign off on the spec." Users "review the spec... edit it... reject it and rewrite it"; the doc "becomes the explicit contract between what you want and what the agent will build," preventing "vibe coding."
key_findings.source_title: Amazon Kiro Review — The Agentic IDE That Writes the Spec Before the Code

key_findings.claim: Progressive disclosure means present overview/metadata first and expand detail only on request (peek/load/extract) — mirroring executive summary plus optional deep dive.
key_findings.evidence: Progressive disclosure is "limiting what is added to the context window to the minimum necessary amount, and adding more detail over time as necessary," implemented via metadata-first listing, selective preloading, and on-demand tools peek_file()/load_file()/extract_file().
key_findings.source_title: Building an internal agent: Progressive disclosure and handling large files

key_findings.claim: Efficient review of agent output is risk-ordered, time-boxed, and surfaces highest-impact checks first; but human judgment is the bottleneck and summaries can mask subtle defects.
key_findings.evidence: A "10-minute progressive review framework": classify (1-2m) → scan CI changes ("highest priority") → check duplicate utilities → trace critical logic → verify security boundaries. Red flags include "agent abandonment — large, unscoped PRs with no implementation plan" and "subtle hallucinations." "Judgment is the bottleneck, and that's fine."
key_findings.source_title: Agent pull requests are everywhere. Here's how to review them.

key_findings.claim: HITL approval design should separate reviewed content ('details') from decision options ('actions'), offer approve/reject/edit, and gate before consequential actions.
key_findings.evidence: "The details key passed into interrupt() contains the generated content, while the action key triggers a handler function." Three pathways: approve, reject, inline-edit. Place interrupts "as a gate before an action" (e.g., before publishing), not after non-deterministic steps.
key_findings.source_title: Building Human-In-The-Loop Agentic Workflows

key_findings.claim: 'Altitude' means keeping plans/instructions at the right abstraction; irrelevant detail degrades both agent and reviewer attention, so use relevance-gated disclosure.
key_findings.evidence: "The more information you have in the file that's not universally applicable... the more likely it is that Claude will ignore your instructions." Tell Claude "how to find important information so that it can find and use it, but only when it needs to." Frontier LLMs follow "~150-200 instructions with reasonable consistency."
key_findings.source_title: Writing a good CLAUDE.md

named_frameworks: Progressive disclosure (three-level model: name/description → full SKILL.md → linked appendix) — Anthropic Agent Skills
Evidence pack (concise-by-default, expandable; action details + context + policy flags + confidence scores + sources + rollback) — StackAI
Specify → Plan → Tasks → Implement phased approval gates — Addy Osmani / spec-driven development
Hierarchical summary / 'bird's-eye view' plan presentation — Addy Osmani
Specification as Quality Gate (3 hypotheses: review against intent, code-against-code circularity, Cynefin domain transition) — Zietsman arXiv
Spec-as-unit-of-work (EARS requirements + acceptance criteria signed off before code) — Amazon Kiro
10-minute progressive review framework (risk-ordered, time-boxed) — GitHub / Andrea Griffiths
Risk-boundary confirmation + dual control (100% high-risk, sample/batch low-risk) — icmd & StackAI
details-vs-actions separation in interrupt() approval (approve/reject/edit) — Kenneth Leung / LangGraph pattern
EARS (Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax)
debates_tensions: Summarized/decision-grade review vs thoroughness: progressive-disclosure advocates (Anthropic, Larson, Osmani, StackAI) optimize fast approval, but GitHub's Griffiths warns 'judgment is the bottleneck' and that summaries can hide subtle hallucinations (compiles but wrong) and unscoped 'agent abandonment.'

Right altitude/unit of review — plan/spec vs code/diff: Zietsman and Kiro argue the spec/intent is the correct review altitude (code-against-code is circular), while GitHub's framework still treats the PR diff as the primary reviewed artifact (validating intent capture alongside it).

How much to gate at all: 'Approve the plan' previews for meaningful changes (icmd) vs sampling/exception-only for low-risk with 100% only at risk boundaries (StackAI) — shared worry is reviewer fatigue/rubber-stamping vs blind spots.

Whether decision-grade summaries should carry explicit confidence scores and provenance: StackAI recommends surfacing confidence scores and cited sources; icmd frames it as verification/receipts rather than numeric confidence — no consensus.
discovery.recency_note: Evidence is very recent and the area is fast-moving: 8 of 10 sources are from 2026 (Feb–May 2026), including GitHub (2026-05-07), StackAI (2026-05-29), icmd (2026-05-19), the Kiro review (2026-05-23), Osmani/O'Reilly (2026-02-20), Zietsman arXiv (2026-03-30), and Kenneth Leung/TDS (2026-03-25). The remaining two are late-2025 (Anthropic 2025-10, Larson 2025-12, HumanLayer 2025-11) and remain foundational/current. No source was older than Oct 2025; nothing here is superseded. Spec-driven approval gates and risk-based 'evidence pack' approval UX are actively converging as of mid-2026.

Consensus is CONTEXTUAL adoption, not universal. The thread states the altitude thesis too strongly.
