Consensus is contextual adoption, qualifying the 'spec is the correct review altitude' claim.

Automation bias / vigilance decrement: a 2025 Cognitive Science review calls vigilance decrement 'one of the most robust findings in attention research' — when systems are mostly correct humans systematically over-trust and it 'cannot be overcome with simple practice or instructions.' 'AI review fatigue'/rubber-stamping is a recognized 2026 problem. The '15-second approval' decision-grade summary is exactly the design that maximizes this bias; the synthesis underweights it.
Verification cost reality check: ~64% of teams report verifying AI code takes as long or longer than writing it from scratch, cutting against the implied throughput gains of summarized review.

Regulatory counter-pressure: EU AI Act Art.14 and NIST AI RMF require demonstrable, trained, provable human oversight for high-risk systems (high-risk track fully operative 2026-08-02), pushing toward MORE substantive review in regulated contexts and qualifying the 'don't review line-by-line' default.

result.title: Making LLMs communicate at the right altitude

discovery.summary: By mid-2026 "communicating at the right altitude" is governed by three converging levers: first-class API verbosity parameters, default model behavior that now favors conciseness, and prompting/config patterns that steer altitude via measurable constraints. The biggest 2025-2026 shift is decoupling how much a model thinks from how much it says: OpenAI GPT-5 (Aug 2025) shipped a `verbosity` param (low/medium/high) separate from `reasoning_effort`, retained in GPT-5.5 as `text.verbosity` (default medium); Google Gemini 3 replaced `thinking_budget` with `thinking_level` and ships less-verbose-by-default; Anthropic Claude Opus 4.8 has no verbosity knob but calibrates length to judged task complexity and exposes an `effort` parameter (low to max). Across vendors the default posture is now terse, so you steer up toward chattiness rather than down toward brevity. The dominant prompting principle is that vague words like "be concise" underperform measurable constraints (word/bullet/section counts, templates) and positive examples of good concision beat negative "don't" instructions; the senior-briefing pattern is persona + audience-level + length cap. Academic work documents a verbosity bias (responses longer than necessary, partly from preference-optimization length bias) and an active length-control research frontier (Plan-and-Write, Hansel, dynamic length feedback).

key_findings.claim: OpenAI GPT-5 (Aug 2025) introduced a dedicated `verbosity` parameter (low/medium/high) that scales output length and depth independently of the prompt, separate from `reasoning_effort` (minimal/medium/high).
key_findings.evidence: Low produced 560 output tokens (terse), medium ~849, high 1,288 with comprehensive commentary; 'reliably scales both the length and depth of the model's output while preserving correctness' without prompt rewrites.
key_findings.source_title: GPT-5 New Params and Tools (OpenAI Cookbook)

key_findings.claim: GPT-5.5 keeps `text.verbosity` (default medium); set it low for concise responses, and define voice and length explicitly in the prompt.
key_findings.evidence: 'For concise responses, use text.verbosity set to low when you prefer shorter, more concise responses'; 'Write for a senior business audience. Keep the answer under 400 words. Use short paragraphs and only include bullets when they improve scannability.' Default to plain paragraphs.
key_findings.source_title: Prompt guidance (OpenAI API Docs)

key_findings.claim: Claude Opus 4.8 calibrates response length to judged task complexity (no verbosity param); to reduce verbosity, add explicit concise instructions and prefer positive examples of good concision over negative 'don't' instructions.
key_findings.evidence: 'Claude Opus 4.8 calibrates response length to how complex it judges the task to be, rather than defaulting to a fixed verbosity.' Recommended snippet: 'Provide concise, focused responses. Skip non-essential context, and keep examples minimal.' 'Positive examples... tend to be more effective than negative examples.'
key_findings.source_title: Prompting best practices (Claude API Docs)

key_findings.claim: Claude Opus 4.8's `effort` parameter (low to max) controls scope/thinking depth; at low/medium it scopes work to exactly what was asked, indirectly controlling altitude. Thinking is off unless adaptive thinking is explicitly enabled.
key_findings.evidence: 'Claude Opus 4.8 respects effort levels strictly, especially at the low end. At low and medium, the model scopes its work to what was asked rather than going above and beyond.'
key_findings.source_title: Prompting best practices (Claude API Docs)

key_findings.claim: Gemini 3 is less verbose by default and uses `thinking_level` (minimal/low/medium/high), which replaces the legacy `thinking_budget`; you must explicitly steer for verbose output.
key_findings.evidence: 'By default, Gemini 3 is less verbose and prefers providing direct, efficient answers.' 'You cannot use both thinking_level and the legacy thinking_budget parameter in the same request.' Keep temperature at default 1.0.
key_findings.source_title: Gemini 3 Developer Guide (generateContent API)

key_findings.claim: Named expert Philipp Schmid recommends an explicit 'Verbosity: [Low/Medium/High]' line in Gemini 3 system constraints plus a dedicated <output_format> section with an executive summary; Gemini 3 can over-analyze overly complex prompt engineering.
key_findings.evidence: 'If you require a more conversational or chatty persona, you must explicitly ask for it.' Output format template: 'Executive Summary: [2 sentence overview]'. Self-review: 'Is the tone authentic to the requested persona?'
key_findings.source_title: Gemini 3 Prompting: Best Practices for General Usage (Philipp Schmid)

key_findings.claim: The dominant cross-vendor prompting principle: 'be concise' is too vague; altitude control works best through measurable, explicit constraints (word/bullet/section counts, templates), structured as directive + constraints + format.
key_findings.evidence: 'Terms like brief, concise, or detailed are subjective; verbosity control works best when it is explicit and measurable through word limits, bullet counts, section counts, or strict templates.' 'Be specific: 5 bullets, each under 15 words not be concise.'
key_findings.source_title: Prompting best practices (Claude API Docs) + 2026 prompt-engineering guides

key_findings.claim: The 'brief me as a senior reviewing a junior' pattern is implemented as persona + audience-level + length constraint (ROLE + TASK + CONTEXT + INSTRUCTIONS).
key_findings.evidence: 'Using persona-based prompting, like You are a senior data scientist... Explain... to a junior developer, helps LLMs provide responses tailored to a specific expertise level and audience.' Example: 'You're a senior legal analyst. Summarize this deal in plain English' under ~150 words.
key_findings.source_title: The Ultimate Guide to Prompt Engineering in 2025 / Module 2: Prompt Structure & Role Engineering (Medium)

key_findings.claim: Claude Code 'output styles' operationalize communication altitude as a persistent system-prompt-level setting: Default (concise), Explanatory (narrated reasoning), Learning (collaborative), plus custom styles.
key_findings.evidence: 'Output styles modify the system prompt directly and apply to every response, giving you fine-grained control over how Claude Code communicates.' Default style 'is concise, and targeted mainly at solving software engineering tasks.'
key_findings.source_title: Output styles (Claude Code Docs)

key_findings.claim: Academic research documents a verbosity bias: LLM responses are significantly longer than necessary, partly an artifact of pairwise preference optimization rewarding length, harming UX and sustainability.
key_findings.evidence: 'LLM-generated responses are significantly longer than target responses for factual questions, regardless of whether targets are human-written or machine-generated.' Title: 'Brevity is the soul of sustainability'; benchmarked 12 LLMs.
key_findings.source_title: Brevity is the soul of sustainability: Characterizing LLM response lengths (arXiv 2506.08686)

key_findings.claim: Dedicated length-control methods (training/decoding level, not prompt level) are an active 2025-2026 frontier: Plan-and-Write, Hansel, precise length control via SFT/PPO/DPO/ORPO, and dynamic length-feedback so models track their own output length.
key_findings.evidence: LLMs 'often struggle with strictly adhering to length constraints when given queries like write a 500-word composition'; feedback mechanisms let LLMs 'track and dynamically adjust their output length.'
key_findings.source_title: Plan-and-Write / dynamic length feedback / Hansel (arXiv length-control papers)

named_frameworks: verbosity parameter (OpenAI GPT-5 / GPT-5.5 text.verbosity: low/medium/high)
reasoning_effort parameter (OpenAI GPT-5: minimal/medium/high)
effort parameter (Anthropic Claude Opus 4.8: low/medium/high/xhigh/max)
adaptive thinking (Claude Opus 4.8, thinking:{type:adaptive})
thinking_level (Google Gemini 3: minimal/low/medium/high; replaces thinking_budget)
Output Styles (Claude Code: Default/Explanatory/Learning + custom)
directive + constraints + format (prompt structure)
ROLE + TASK + CONTEXT + INSTRUCTIONS (persona/audience framing)
Plan-and-Write (structure-guided length control)
Hansel (output-length controlling framework)
dynamic length-feedback (output-length tracking)
LLM-Lingua (prompt compression)
debates_tensions: Vendor docs (Claude Opus 4.8, Gemini 3) claim the newest models self-calibrate length and advise REMOVING brevity/structure scaffolding, while academic and practitioner sources still treat verbosity bias as a live, measurable problem requiring active constraint. Reconciliation: vendor claims are specific to the latest generation; the literature benchmarks broader/older model populations.

Parameter vs. prompt: OpenAI and Google expose explicit verbosity/thinking knobs and prefer them over prompt rewrites; Anthropic deliberately ships NO verbosity parameter, relying on task-complexity calibration + concision examples + the effort lever. 'Right altitude control' is vendor-specific.

Reasoning depth is NOT output verbosity: GPT-5 explicitly separates them (reason hard, answer tersely), while Gemini folds reasoning into thinking_level and handles output verbosity only via prompt steering — a common point of practitioner confusion.
Negative vs. positive instruction: Claude docs say positive examples of good concision beat negative 'don't over-explain' instructions, contradicting common practitioner reliance on prohibitions.

Format defaults: OpenAI now advises plain paragraphs as default and bullets only when they aid comprehension, pushing back on the habitual always-bullet output style — a subtle altitude question (structure can summarize or fragment).
discovery.recency_note: Evidence is very recent and the area is fast-moving. The strongest sources are 2025-2026 primary vendor docs: Gemini 3 developer guide last updated 2026-05-29, OpenAI GPT-5.5 prompt guidance (2026), Claude Opus 4.8 prompting best practices (2026), OpenAI GPT-5 cookbook (2025-08-07), and Philipp Schmid's Gemini 3 guide (2025-11-19). Academic verbosity/length-control papers span 2024-12 through 2026. The verbosity-parameter and thinking-level/effort landscape changes with every model release, so specifics (defaults, value names) should be re-verified against vendor docs at time of use.

verdicts.claim: OpenAI GPT-5 (Aug 2025) introduced a dedicated `verbosity` parameter (low/medium/high) that scales output length and depth independently of the prompt, separate from `reasoning_effort`.
verdicts.note: Verbatim quote present: 'reliably scales both the length and depth ... without changing the underlying prompt.' Caveat: the cited token counts (560/849/1288) are example-specific; another rendering of the same doc shows 731/1017/1263. The directional claim holds but the exact numbers are illustrative, not a spec.

verdicts.claim: GPT-5.5 keeps `text.verbosity` (default medium); set low for concise responses; default to plain paragraphs; example 'Write for a senior business audience. Keep the answer under 400 words.'
verdicts.note: All elements verbatim in the GPT-5.5 section. Doc has no explicit publish date but covers GPT-5.5/5.4/5.3-Codex etc., consistent with 2026.

verdicts.claim: Claude Opus 4.8 calibrates response length to judged task complexity (no verbosity param); prefer positive concision examples over negative 'don't' instructions; snippet 'Provide concise, focused responses. Skip non-essential context, and keep examples minimal.'
verdicts.note: All three elements verbatim. Page uses a <NextOpus /> placeholder that resolves to Claude Opus 4.8 (confirmed via anchor #prompting-claude-opus-4-8 and model-string examples claude-opus-4-8).

verdicts.claim: Claude Opus 4.8's `effort` parameter (low to max) controls scope/thinking depth; at low/medium it scopes work to exactly what was asked, indirectly controlling altitude; thinking off unless adaptive thinking explicitly enabled.
verdicts.note: Quotes verbatim and five effort levels (low/medium/high/xhigh/max) confirmed. Skeptic caveat: the thread frames low/medium effort as the lever for less-verbose RESPONSES, conflating scope-of-work/thinking-depth with output verbosity. The doc keeps these in separate sections and actually warns against low effort (under-thinking) and recommends starting at xhigh/high. Effort is a cost/intelligence knob, not a verbosity knob.

verdicts.claim: Gemini 3 is less verbose by default and uses `thinking_level` (minimal/low/medium/high), which replaces the legacy `thinking_budget`; cannot use both in one request; keep temperature 1.0.
verdicts.note: All elements verbatim; doc last updated 2026-05-29. 400-error on using both params confirmed.

verdicts.claim: Philipp Schmid recommends an explicit 'Verbosity: [Low/Medium/High]' line and an <output_format> Executive Summary section; warns Gemini 3 can over-analyze overly complex prompt engineering.
verdicts.note: Verbosity line, <output_format>/Executive Summary, and 'must explicitly ask for a chatty persona' all confirmed (post dated 2025-11-19). NOT confirmed: the 'may over-analyze verbose or overly complex prompt engineering' warning was not found in the post. That sub-claim should be dropped or re-sourced.

verdicts.claim: Dominant cross-vendor principle: 'be concise' is too vague; altitude control works best through measurable explicit constraints (word/bullet/section counts, templates) — quote attributed to Claude API docs.
verdicts.note: The principle is sound and widely echoed, but the marquee quote ('Terms like brief, concise, or detailed are subjective ... word limits, bullet counts, section counts, or strict templates') does NOT appear in the Claude doc it is primarily cited to. The doc says 'Be specific about the desired output format and constraints' and 'tell Claude what to do instead of what not to do.' The quote is third-party aggregation (DigitalOcean/Lakera), which the finding partly admits. Treat as practitioner consensus, not a vendor quote.

verdicts.claim: The 'brief me as a senior reviewing a junior' pattern = persona + audience-level + length constraint (ROLE + TASK + CONTEXT + INSTRUCTIONS).
verdicts.note: Sole source is a generic Medium listicle. The pattern is uncontroversial but the framing reads like generic prompt-engineering boilerplate without an authoritative source. Confidence 'medium' in the thread is appropriate; should not be elevated.

verdicts.claim: Claude Code 'output styles' operationalize altitude as a persistent system-prompt setting: Default (concise), Explanatory, Learning, plus custom styles.
verdicts.note: Core claim ('modify the system prompt directly and apply to every response') is verbatim-correct. STALE specifics: current docs list FOUR built-ins — Default, Proactive, Explanatory, Learning — not three. The /output-style command (and /output-style:new) were deprecated v2.1.73 and removed v2.1.91; selection is now via /config. Custom-style frontmatter also expanded (keep-coding-instructions, force-for-plugin).

verdicts.claim: Academic research (arXiv 2506.08686) documents a verbosity bias: responses significantly longer than necessary, partly an artifact of pairwise preference optimization rewarding length; benchmarked 12 LLMs; responses longer than human-written or machine-generated targets.
verdicts.note: Confirmed in 2506.08686: title, author list, 12 LLMs across 5 datasets, ACL 2025 Findings, 'substantially longer than necessary.' NOT in that paper's abstract: the 'pairwise preference optimization rewards length' causal attribution and the 'human-written or machine-generated targets' quote. Those belong to the broader DPO/RLHF length-bias literature, so the claim is true of the field but the citation is mis-attributed.

verdicts.claim: Dedicated length-control methods (training/decoding level) are an active 2025-2026 frontier: Plan-and-Write, Hansel, precise length control, dynamic length-feedback.
verdicts.note: Plan-and-Write (2511.01807, Nov 3 2025) confirmed: structure-guided length control without retraining via planning + word-counting. Cluster (Hansel 2412.14033, dynamic feedback 2601.01768, precise control 2412.11937) is real and recent. Minor: the exact 'write a 500-word composition' phrasing wasn't located in the Plan-and-Write abstract, but the under-addressed length-control framing is present.

verdicts.claim: Cross-vendor thesis: terseness is now the default posture across frontier models, so you steer UP toward chattiness rather than DOWN toward brevity.
verdicts.note: YapBench (arXiv 2601.00624, Jan 2026, 76 models incl. OpenAI/Anthropic/Google) finds the OPPOSITE population-level trend: models released 2025-2026 are on average MORE verbose than 2023-2024 models ('length bias is getting worse, not better'); worst models over-generate 10-20x; GPT-3.5-Turbo is among the most concise. The thread's 'terse-by-default, steer up' generalization is a per-vendor marketing posture, not a measured cross-vendor fact.

training_data_smells: Finding 8 (persona / ROLE+TASK+CONTEXT+INSTRUCTIONS 'senior reviewing a junior') is sourced only to a generic Medium 'Ultimate Guide to Prompt Engineering 2025' listicle; reads like prompt-engineering boilerplate that could be produced from training-data priors rather than a primary source.

Finding 7's marquee quote ('5 bullets, each under 15 words not be concise'; 'word limits, bullet counts, section counts, or strict templates') is presented as if vendor-sourced (Claude docs) but is actually third-party SEO-style aggregation; true in spirit, weak in provenance.

The synthesis-level generalization 'terseness is now the default posture across vendors; steer UP not DOWN' is asserted more confidently than the evidence supports — vendor docs say it only about their own latest models, and it is contradicted as a cross-vendor trend by YapBench 2026.
contradicting_evidence: YapBench (arXiv 2601.00624, Jan 2026; tabularis.ai), benchmarking 76 assistant LLMs across OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/Meta/Mistral: models released in 2025-2026 are on AVERAGE MORE verbose than 2023-2024 models ('length bias in training is getting worse, not better'); the most verbose models over-generate 10-20x; GPT-3.5-Turbo is among the most concise. Directly qualifies the thread's 'terse-by-default' thesis.

GPT-5's verbosity/reasoning_effort parameters have documented integration failures: Azure does not support them on the standard gpt-5-chat model (2025-04-01-preview), and LangChain has a bug where verbosity conflicts with structured output (GitHub issue #32492). The 'just set the first-class knob' framing omits these caveats.

Verbosity-compensation literature (arXiv 2411.07858 'Verbosity != Veracity') shows over-length responses persist EVEN UNDER explicit conciseness instructions, with verbosity-compensation frequencies of 13.6% (Llama-3-70B) to 74% (Mistral-7B) — qualifies the optimistic 'measurable constraints solve verbosity' framing.
GPT-5 cookbook token counts are not stable across renderings (560/849/1288 in one, 731/1017/1263 in another), indicating the numeric 'scaling' is anecdotal per-prompt rather than a guaranteed spec.

result.title: Trust calibration — automation bias vs verification fatigue

discovery.summary: The 2025-2026 evidence shows AI coding tools shift the bottleneck from writing to judging, and humans miscalibrate that judgment in both directions. UNDER-reviewing: Sonar (Jan 2026, 1,100+ devs) found 96% distrust AI code but only 48% always verify it ("verification debt," per AWS CTO Werner Vogels); the foundational Parasuraman & Manzey result (operators of high-reliability automation are ~50% less likely to detect failures) underlies this rubber-stamping; an Anthropic RCT (Feb 2026) found full delegation cut comprehension ~17 points (50% vs 67%), with code-generators scoring <40% vs >65% for conceptual users; and CHI '26 confirms over-reliance is the dominant inappropriate-reliance mode. OVER-reviewing: 38% say reviewing AI code is harder than human code, AI PRs carry ~1.7x more issues (CodeRabbit, 10.83 vs 6.45/PR), and the resulting "density of work"/decision fatigue makes reviewers sloppy. Humans add most value on intent, business logic, and architecture (AI is reliable only for mechanical/security/style defects); Anthropic reports devs "fully delegate" only 0-20% of tasks and delegate only the "easily verifiable." Best mitigations are trust-adaptive interventions (counter-explanations + forced pauses: 38% less inappropriate reliance, 20% accuracy gain) and specification-grounded review that breaks the circular AI-reviews-itself loop (90.9% adoption lift). Sources diverge on net effect — METR found experienced devs 19% slower (and misperceived a 20% speedup) while DORA 2025 finds AI raises throughput but also instability.

key_findings.claim: The verification gap: 96% of developers don't fully trust AI code, yet only 48% always verify it before committing; 42% of committed code is now AI-produced.
key_findings.evidence: 96% of developers do not fully trust that AI-generated code is functionally correct... only 48% always verify their AI-assisted code before committing it. 42% of committed code comes from AI.
key_findings.source_title: Sonar Data Reveals Critical Verification Gap in AI Coding

key_findings.claim: 'Verification debt' is the named risk for unreviewed AI code; fewer than half of developers review AI code before committing, and 61% agree AI produces code that looks correct but isn't reliable.
key_findings.evidence: fewer than half say they review it before committing... 'verification debt' [term] used by AWS CTO Werner Vogels... 61% of respondents agreed AI often produces code that looks correct but isn't reliable.
key_findings.source_title: Nearly half of software developers don't check AI-generated code (IT Pro)

key_findings.claim: Foundational automation-complacency result: operators of constantly high-reliability automation are ~50% less likely to detect failures; complacency occurs under multi-task load and afflicts both novices and experts.
key_findings.evidence: operators of constantly high reliability systems were 50% less likely to detect failures than operators of unreliable systems... found in both naive and expert participants and cannot be overcome with simple practice.
key_findings.source_title: Complacency and Bias in Human Use of Automation: An Attentional Integration (Parasuraman & Manzey)

key_findings.claim: Over-reliance is the dominant inappropriate-reliance failure mode across HCI human-AI decision-making studies; people over-rely without analytically engaging, and engagement comes at a cost.
key_findings.evidence: recent empirical studies show critical concerns that people over-rely on AI advice without analytically engaging with it... user engagement is a precious commodity... it comes at a cost.
key_findings.source_title: Do People Appropriately Rely on AI-Advice? An Analytical Review of HCI Research on Human-AI Decision-Making (CHI 2026)

key_findings.claim: Full delegation to AI atrophies skill ~17 points: AI group scored 50% vs 67% manual on comprehension/debugging quizzes; conceptual users scored >=65% while code-delegators scored <40%.
key_findings.evidence: The AI group averaged 50% compared to 67% for the manual coding group... those who used AI for conceptual questions scored 65% or higher, while those delegating code generation to AI scored below 40%.
key_findings.source_title: Anthropic Study: AI Coding Assistance Reduces Developer Skill Mastery by 17% (InfoQ, reporting Shen & Tamkin)

key_findings.claim: Reviewing AI code is harder than reviewing human code (38% of devs), shifting and intensifying the burden onto reviewers.
key_findings.evidence: 38% of developers say reviewing AI-generated code requires more effort than reviewing code written by their human colleagues... 'Reviewing [another's] code is so much harder than writing it. AI tools are increasing the rate at which people can churn out code that needs to be reviewed.'
key_findings.source_title: Balancing AI tensions: Moving from AI adoption to effective SDLC use (DORA)

key_findings.claim: AI-co-authored PRs contain ~1.7x more issues than human-only PRs (10.83 vs 6.45 per PR), with security issues up to 2.74x and logic/correctness +75% — more defects to catch, that look correct at a glance.
key_findings.evidence: AI-authored changes produced 10.83 issues per PR, compared to 6.45 for human-only PRs (~1.7x)... security vulnerabilities up to 2.74x higher, logic/correctness 75% more common. AI optimizes for surface-level correctness, not deep project context.
key_findings.source_title: State of AI vs Human Code Generation Report (CodeRabbit)

key_findings.claim: Verification/decision fatigue: AI lowered writing cost but raised review cognitive load, creating a 'density of work' that makes reviewers sloppy and error-prone from burnout.
key_findings.evidence: this imbalance leads to decision fatigue, increasing the risk of human error when reviewers become sloppy due to burnout... developers spend more time gathering context and making high-stakes judgement calls than actually coding.
key_findings.source_title: Coding Agents Are Giving Everyone Decision Fatigue (Dev|Journal)

key_findings.claim: METR RCT: experienced developers were 19% SLOWER with AI on familiar mature repos, yet expected a 24% speedup and still believed AI sped them up 20% afterward — a large self-perception miscalibration.
key_findings.evidence: Developers took 19% longer when using AI tools... Developers expected 24% speedup, perceived 20% speedup post-study, but actually experienced slowdown. 16 developers, 246 issues, repos averaging 22k+ stars and 1M+ LOC.
key_findings.source_title: Measuring the Impact of Early-2025 AI on Experienced Open-Source Developer Productivity (METR)

key_findings.claim: Humans add most value on intent, business logic, and architecture; AI review is reliable only for mechanical/pattern defects (security signatures, null safety, style). 84% use AI but only ~33% trust its accuracy.
key_findings.evidence: AI is highly accurate for security vulnerabilities and null safety but less reliable for business logic and architectural concerns... humans remain essential for business intent, system context, architectural implications, and compliance. 84% of developers now use AI; only a third trust the accuracy.
key_findings.source_title: How Accurate Is AI Code Review in 2026? (CodeAnt AI)

key_findings.claim: Anthropic frames human oversight as scaling 'through intelligent failures': engineers can 'fully delegate' only 0-20% of tasks, keep design-dependent/conceptually difficult work, and delegate freely only what is 'easily verifiable' (sniff-checkable). The role shifts from writing to reviewing/supervising/validating.
key_findings.evidence: they report being able to 'fully delegate' only 0-20% of tasks... delegate tasks that are easily verifiable, where they 'can relatively easily sniff-check on correctness'... the shift is from writing code to reviewing, prompting, active supervision, validation, and human judgment... make human attention count where it matters most.
key_findings.source_title: 2026 Agentic Coding Trends Report (Anthropic)

key_findings.claim: Trust-adaptive interventions fix BOTH failure modes: counter-explanations at high trust and supporting-explanations at low trust yield up to 38% reduction in inappropriate reliance and 20% accuracy gain; forced pauses reduce over-reliance. Under-reliance is also costly — at low trust, doctors reject correct AI diagnoses 68% of the time (up from 40%).
key_findings.evidence: providing supporting and counter-explanations during moments of low and high trust... yields up to 38% reduction in inappropriate reliance and 20% improvement in decision accuracy... reduce over-reliance by adaptively inserting forced pauses... when user trust is low, doctors reject correct AI diagnoses 68% of the time, up from 40% otherwise.
key_findings.source_title: Adjust for Trust: Mitigating Trust-Induced Inappropriate Reliance on AI Assistance (Srinivasan & Thomason, IUI '26)

key_findings.claim: Specification-grounded review breaks the circular 'AI reviews its own output' loop: homogeneous LLM pipelines have correlated errors and overcorrection bias; grounding review in human-authored specs lifted developer adoption of suggestions by 90.9%.
key_findings.evidence: homogeneous LLM pipelines exhibit correlated errors... AI checks code against itself, not against intent... specification-grounded review achieved a 90.9% improvement in adoption rates (Wang et al.).
key_findings.source_title: The Specification as Quality Gate: Three Hypotheses on AI-Assisted Code Review (Zietsman)

key_findings.claim: The 'augmentation trap': even a rational worker who fully anticipates skill erosion adopts AI because front-loaded productivity outweighs long-run skill costs, ending up less capable; less-experienced workers can deskill permanently.
key_findings.evidence: even a decision-maker who fully anticipates skill erosion rationally adopts AI when front-loaded productivity gains outweigh long-run skill costs... less experienced workers deskill permanently, widening inequality irreversibly.
key_findings.source_title: The Augmentation Trap: AI Productivity and the Cost of Cognitive Offloading (Caosun & Aral)

key_findings.claim: DORA 2025: AI adoption (~90%) now correlates with higher delivery throughput but continues to correlate with lower delivery stability; 30% of developers report little/no trust in AI code. AI acts as an amplifier of existing team capability.
key_findings.evidence: higher AI adoption is associated with an increase in both software delivery throughput and software delivery instability... 30% of developers report little to no trust in the code generated by AI.
key_findings.source_title: Announcing the 2025 DORA Report (Google Cloud / DORA)

named_frameworks: Automation complacency / automation bias — attentional integration model (Parasuraman & Manzey, 2010; omission vs commission errors)
Appropriate reliance / over-reliance / under-reliance; Relative AI Reliance (RAIR) — HCI human-AI decision-making literature (CHI 2026 review)
Verification gap / verification debt (Sonar 2026; 'verification debt' per AWS CTO Werner Vogels)
The Augmentation Trap — alpha skill-neutral vs beta knowledge-complementary gains; steady-state loss; skill divergence (Caosun & Aral 2026)
Vibe-Check Protocol — quantifying cognitive offloading in AI programming (Aiersilan 2026)
Specification as Quality Gate; Correlated Error Hypothesis; Circular Review Problem; overcorrection bias (Zietsman 2026)
Trust-adaptive explanations / cognitive forcing functions — forced pauses + adaptive counter-explanations (Srinivasan & Thomason, 'Adjust for Trust', IUI 2026)
'Human oversight scales through intelligent failures'; delegate-the-sniff-checkable heuristic; fully-delegate-only-0-20% (Anthropic 2026 Agentic Coding Trends Report)
AI-as-amplifier — throughput up, stability down (DORA 2025)
debates_tensions: Net productivity: METR's RCT found experienced devs 19% SLOWER with AI on familiar mature codebases, while DORA 2025 finds AI now RAISES throughput at scale — though both agree AI raises instability and pushes work downstream into review.

More AI reviewers as the cure vs. a circular trap: industry (CodeRabbit) deploys AI PR review gates, but Zietsman argues homogeneous AI-on-AI review is structurally circular, amplifying correlated errors unless grounded in a human-authored spec.

Is over- or under-reliance the bigger danger? CHI '26 frames over-reliance as dominant; 'Adjust for Trust' shows under-reliance is equally costly (doctors reject correct AI advice 68% of the time at low trust) — the real target is calibration, not maximizing or minimizing reliance.

Does AI build or erode skill? Anthropic and the Augmentation Trap show delegation atrophies skill, but conceptual/engaged use preserves or builds it — the determinant is HOW AI is used, not adoption itself.

Source-incentive caution: vendor reports (Sonar, CodeRabbit, CodeAnt, Anthropic) sell review/verification tooling; their directional findings align with independent academic work (METR, CHI, IUI, MIT) but exact magnitudes should be read as vendor-favorable.
discovery.recency_note: Evidence is very recent and the area is fast-moving. The strongest sources cluster in late 2025 through May 2026: peer-reviewed/preprint work (CHI 2026, IUI 2026 'Adjust for Trust', Zietsman spec-as-quality-gate 2026, Caosun & Aral 2026, Vibe-Check 2026), the METR RCT (Jul 2025), the 2025 DORA report, Sonar's Jan 2026 survey, CodeRabbit's Dec 2025 report, and Anthropic's 2026 Agentic Coding Trends Report. Only one source is foundational/older (Parasuraman & Manzey 2010), explicitly flagged as such and extended — not superseded — by the 2025-2026 work. The named taxonomies (RAIR, verification debt, augmentation trap, trust-adaptive cognitive forcing) are emerging and likely to be refined within months.

verdicts.claim: METR RCT: experienced devs 19% slower with AI; expected 24% speedup, perceived 20% speedup; 16 devs, 246 issues, repos 22k+ stars / 1M+ LOC, Cursor Pro + Claude 3.5/3.7 Sonnet.
verdicts.note: All figures re-fetch exactly. BUT METR's own 2026-02-24 follow-up found the same developers ~18% FASTER and is redesigning the study due to selection bias. The thread uses the 19%-slowdown as a stable tension-pole without disclosing the authors' walk-back. Quoted correctly; used in an outdated way.

verdicts.claim: Sonar verification gap: 96% don't fully trust AI code, only 48% always verify, 42% of committed code is AI-produced.
verdicts.note: All three figures verbatim; 1,100+ devs; Jan 8 2026.

verdicts.claim: 61% agreed AI often produces code that looks correct but isn't reliable.
verdicts.note: IT Pro confirms 61% for one worded statement. Sonar's own press release reports 53% citing 'code that looks correct but isn't reliable' as a negative impact. Likely two different survey items; unreconciled internal tension. 'Verification debt'/Werner Vogels attribution confirmed.

verdicts.claim: Anthropic RCT: full delegation cut comprehension ~17 pts (50% vs 67%); conceptual users >=65%, code-delegators <40%; speed gain not statistically significant; 52 junior engineers, Trio library.
verdicts.note: Every figure confirmed via InfoQ. Note this is a learning/skill-formation study on juniors, not a code-review-bias study; thread applies it appropriately to the skill-atrophy point.

verdicts.claim: AI-co-authored PRs ~1.7x more issues (10.83 vs 6.45), security up to 2.74x, logic +75%.
verdicts.note: All figures confirmed; 470 PRs (320 AI / 150 human); Dec 17 2025. Caveat the thread under-weights: AI-authorship was INFERRED from signals, not confirmed; vendor sells AI review tooling. Directionally credible, magnitudes vendor-favorable.

verdicts.claim: Parasuraman & Manzey: operators of high-reliability automation ~50% less likely to detect failures; complacency under multi-task load; in both naive and expert participants; not fixed by simple practice.
verdicts.note: Abstract confirms multi-task-load, naive+expert, and not-fixed-by-practice verbatim. The specific '50% less likely to detect failures' figure is NOT in the abstract — it is a body summary of a single reviewed study, frequently quoted secondhand. Mechanism solid; the 50% number loosely attributed. Correctly flagged as foundational/2010.

verdicts.claim: Adjust for Trust (Srinivasan & Thomason, IUI '26): supporting/counter-explanations yield up to 38% reduction in inappropriate reliance and 20% accuracy gain; forced pauses reduce over-reliance; doctors reject correct AI 68% vs 40% at low trust.
verdicts.note: Title, authors, and the 38%/20% + forced-pauses findings confirmed verbatim from the abstract. The '68% vs 40%' doctor stat is in the full body, not the abstract — unverified here but plausible and consistent.

verdicts.claim: DORA 2025: ~90% AI adoption; throughput now positively associated (reversal); stability still negatively associated; AI-as-amplifier; 30% report little/no trust.
verdicts.note: All claims confirmed verbatim including the explicit throughput reversal and amplifier framing.

verdicts.claim: 38% of developers say reviewing AI-generated code requires more effort than reviewing human colleagues' code (attributed to DORA).
verdicts.note: The 38% figure is NOT on the dora.dev page (which has only the qualitative 'babysitting the AI' / 'reviewing is harder than writing' quotes). The 38% is a Sonar survey figure (confirmed via IT Pro). Real number, mis-sourced to DORA.

verdicts.claim: Specification-grounded review breaks the circular AI-reviews-itself loop; grounding in human specs lifted developer adoption of suggestions by 90.9% (Wang et al., per Zietsman).
verdicts.note: Title (The Specification as Quality Gate), author (Christo Zietsman), and the correlated-errors/circular-review thesis confirmed from abstract. BUT the '90.9% adoption lift (Wang et al.)' could not be located anywhere in the paper — weakest single claim, treat as unverified. Also: this is a hypotheses/preprint paper, so even the core thesis is proposed, not demonstrated.

verdicts.claim: CHI '26 analytical review confirms over-reliance is the dominant inappropriate-reliance failure mode across HCI human-AI decision-making studies.
verdicts.note: ACM DL returned HTTP 403; paper existence/wording not directly confirmable. Broader literature confirms over-reliance is a major documented concern but treats under-reliance/algorithm-aversion as co-equal — so the 'dominant' characterization is contestable, not settled. Thread already marked confidence medium.

verdicts.claim: The Augmentation Trap (Caosun & Aral, MIT): rational AI adoption still erodes long-run skill; less-experienced workers deskill permanently.
verdicts.note: Not independently re-fetched this pass; arXiv ID format plausible for Apr 2026. Critically, this is a THEORETICAL economic model result, not empirical evidence of real-world deskilling — should be read as a formal argument, not data. Thread marks it medium.

contradicting_evidence: METR's own 2026-02-24 follow-up (metr.org/blog/2026-02-24-uplift-update/) found the same developers ~18% FASTER with AI, directly reversing the 19%-slowdown headline; METR is redesigning the experiment due to selection bias (devs refused to work without AI; 30-50% avoided submitting high-AI-benefit tasks). The thread relies on the slowdown figure without this walk-back.

A 'modest gains' cluster of 2026 sources (Second Talent: 84% adoption but only ~10% productivity gain; philippdubach: 93% adoption, productivity 'hasn't moved') places real-world net effect as small-positive — between METR-slowdown and DORA-throughput, so neither extreme dominates.

The reliance literature (Computers in Human Behavior 2024 doi 10.1016/j.chb.2024.108352; arXiv 2509.08010; CHI 2025 doi 10.1145/3706598.3714097) treats over- and under-reliance (algorithm aversion) as CO-EQUAL failure modes with a dynamic flip (overtrust then over-distrust after a visible error), undercutting any 'over-reliance is THE dominant mode' framing while supporting the thread's calibration thesis.

The skill effect is conditional, not a simple deskilling story: 2026 sources (Uvik; Data Science Collective; the Anthropic study itself) confirm conceptual/engaged use preserves or builds skill while only careless delegation erodes it — corroborating the thread's 'how, not whether' nuance and arguing against a blanket deskilling narrative.
training_data_smells: Zietsman's '90.9% improvement in adoption attributed to Wang et al.' — precise statistic not locatable anywhere in the cited Zietsman paper; classic over-specific, weakly-grounded figure shape. Highest smell.

Parasuraman & Manzey '50% less likely to detect failures' — real but NOT in the abstract; a secondhand body-of-paper summary of a single reviewed study, showing mild citation-telephone smell despite the source being genuine and foundational.

'Vibe-Check Protocol' (Aiersilan, arXiv 2601.02410) — appears only in the raw file, not independently re-fetched; niche preprint with a catchy name that warrants a direct existence check before being treated as load-bearing.

discovery.summary: The junior-developer analogy is a useful delegation posture but breaks on no cross-session learning, confidently wrong, jagged capability, and no accountability. Full synthesis and all 10 sources are in the raw file.

key_findings.claim: Practitioners treat LLM output like a junior dev but verify every output; Harris counters it is NOT a junior (no memory, no consequences); Goedecke notes no learning across sessions.
key_findings.evidence: I treat every AI-generated snippet as if it came from a junior developer: I read, run, and test it. I am the senior dev.
key_findings.source_title: My LLM coding workflow going into 2026 (Osmani)

key_findings.claim: Jaggedness is structural and over-trust is empirical: Gemini is plus 1.99 SD math but minus 1.02 SD visual reasoning, and devs were 19 percent slower with AI yet believed they were 20 percent faster.
key_findings.evidence: Allowing AI increased completion time by 19 percent; developers estimated a 20 percent reduction (METR). Gemini 2.5 Pro jaggedness per DeepMind.
key_findings.source_title: METR (Willison) and Characterizing Model Jaggedness (Google DeepMind)

named_frameworks: Jaggedness index (Google DeepMind 2026)
Jagged frontier; Centaur vs Cyborg (Mollick)
Situational Leadership (Hersey-Blanchard)
7 Levels of Delegation / Delegation Poker (Appelo)
Tannenbaum-Schmidt continuum
Agentic AI Maturity Model (Microsoft 2026)
The Accountability Stack (Yamin)
debates_tensions: Useful posture vs harmful metaphor (Osmani/Goedecke vs Harris).
Trust accrues vs resets: frameworks assume graduation, but LLMs do not learn across sessions.
Speedup vs slowdown: METR 19 percent slowdown vs BCG large gains on in-frontier tasks.
Can agents be accountable: Yamin no; HBR/Microsoft say engineered controls substitute.
discovery.recency_note: 6 of 10 sources from 2026; METR mid-2025; Mollick 2023 and Appelo flagged foundational. Fast-moving area.

verdicts.claim: Osmani treats every AI snippet 'as if it came from a junior developer' (reads/runs/tests it) and remains the accountable senior engineer.
verdicts.note: Exact quotes verified in source dated 2026-01-04: 'I treat every AI-generated snippet as if it came from a junior developer...', 'I remain the accountable engineer', 'I am the senior dev'. Also the Willison 'over-confident and prone to mistakes' / 'writes code with complete conviction - including bugs or nonsense' framing.

verdicts.claim: Osmani explicitly contrasts LLMs with junior devs as 'lack judgment, no accountability, cannot learn from corrections across sessions, require verification of every output rather than developing trustworthiness over time'.
verdicts.note: Judgment / accountability / verify-everything are supported. The 'cannot learn across sessions / never develops trustworthiness over time' contrast is NOT explicitly stated by Osmani; it is the discovery agent's synthesis (sourced elsewhere from Harris/Goedecke) presented as an Osmani-attributed paraphrase. Down-rated from high.

verdicts.claim: METR RCT found AI increased completion time 19% while developers estimated a ~20% reduction (16 devs, 143 hrs of screen recording).
verdicts.note: Figures verified as originally reported (2025-07-12). But METR itself (2026-02-24) acknowledged severe selection effects — devs who benefit most declined the no-AI condition; 30-50% withheld tasks they didn't want to do without AI — biasing the estimate DOWNWARD. METR now calls 19% a lower bound and is redesigning the study. The thread uses this as its 'strongest empirical caution'; that use no longer holds un-caveated.

verdicts.claim: Google DeepMind jaggedness paper: Gemini 2.5 Pro scores z=+1.99 on AIME 2025 math (top ~3%) and z=-1.02 on ARC-AGI-1 visual reasoning (bottom ~16%); jaggedness is 'alien to our own' and a structural property; index = SD of Winsorized z-scores.
verdicts.note: Verified verbatim from the PDF (Morris et al., Google DeepMind, 2026-01-27): z'_math=1.99, z'_visual_reasoning=-1.02, top 3% / bottom 16%, 'alien to our own', 'jaggedness is a structural property of current architectures and scaling paradigms', index = spread of Winsorized z-scores. Caveat: paper states these numbers are 'illustrative of the method and not as an endorsement of these particular benchmarks' — the thread presents them as a hard capability fact.

verdicts.claim: BCG/HBS field experiment of 758 consultants: 12.2% more tasks / 25.1% faster / 40% higher quality in-frontier; on an out-of-frontier task humans were right 84% unaided vs 60-70% with AI.
verdicts.note: All figures and centaur/cyborg definitions verified in the cited Mollick post. But this is a 2023-09-16 source reporting the 2023 Dell'Acqua et al. study — foundational, not recent. Correctly flagged as foundational by the discovery agent; usable as 'what the study found', dated as live 2026 evidence.

verdicts.claim: Harris argues the LLM is NOT a junior engineer (no long-term memory, won't mature, no legal/professional consequences, no internalized values); compares it to Amelia Bedelia and Leonard Shelby from Memento.
verdicts.note: All quotes verified in source dated 2026-04-29. The Amelia Bedelia / Memento comparison is verbatim.

verdicts.claim: Goedecke uses agents 'constantly and with light supervision', ~30s initial assessment, rejects most attempts, and notes agents show 'no learning' across sessions.
verdicts.note: 'constantly and with light supervision', '~thirty seconds', 'reject them entirely' all verified verbatim (2026-05-17). The 'no learning across sessions' attribution is NOT explicit in the article; it is implied via 'five or six (or more!) agent attempts' and over-attributed by the discovery agent as a direct claim.

verdicts.claim: Yamin: 'literally nobody's accountable', 'personalized responsibility becomes mathematically unattainable', reviewers degrade to ritual, and manipulated chain-of-thought 'inflated false positives by 90%'.
verdicts.note: All phrases verified present in the Medium piece (2026-01-25). But these are second-hand citations (Romanchuk & Bondar 2026; Khalifa et al. arXiv:2601.14691, 800 trajectories) inside an opinion post; the underlying primary sources and the 90% figure were NOT independently verified. Treat as illustrative, not established fact. Discovery agent's 'medium' confidence is appropriate.

verdicts.claim: LLMs structurally cannot learn across sessions, so trust must reset per session rather than graduate.
verdicts.note: True of the bare/stateless model. Increasingly contested for deployed agent SYSTEMS in 2026: Mem0 (MCP-integrated with Claude Code), AgeMem (arXiv:2601.01885), survey arXiv:2603.07670 show engineered external-memory layers persisting learned facts/policies across sessions. The thread treats no-cross-session-learning as a hard invariant; better framed as a model default that memory layers partially close (with staleness/identity caveats).

contradicting_evidence: METR's own follow-up (https://metr.org/blog/2026-02-24-uplift-update/, 2026-02-24) acknowledges severe selection effects in the 19%-slowdown RCT: developers who benefit most from AI declined the no-AI condition, and 30-50% of developers withheld tasks they didn't want to do without AI. METR now calls the 19% figure a LOWER BOUND on AI's true productivity effect and is redesigning the study. This directly undercuts the thread's use of '19% slower' as its strongest empirical caution against over-trusting agents.

A substantial 2026 agentic-memory literature contradicts the flat 'no cross-session learning' pillar for deployed agent systems: Mem0 (MCP-integrated with Claude Code, https://mem0.ai/blog/state-of-ai-agent-memory-2026), AgeMem (arXiv:2601.01885), and survey arXiv:2603.07670 show production agents persisting learned facts and policies across sessions via external memory stores. The bare model is stateless, but the engineered system increasingly is not — so 'trust never compounds' is weaker than stated, though memory staleness and cross-session identity remain open problems.

The junior-dev analogy has active 2026 defenders beyond Osmani/Goedecke (rmoff 'Reflections of a Developer on LLMs in January 2026', 2026-01-27; arXiv:2602.00496 'From Junior to Senior: Allocating Agency...'), treating 'eager junior under close supervision' as a working and pedagogically useful model. Harris's 'the analogy is harmful/insulting' is one pole of a live debate, not a settled refutation.

The DeepMind paper explicitly states the Gemini 2.5 Pro z-scores (+1.99 / -1.02) are 'illustrative of the method and not as an endorsement of these particular benchmarks' — the thread presents them as a hard capability claim rather than the worked example the authors intended.
training_data_smells: Finding 2 attributes a tidy four-part contrast (lack judgment / no accountability / cannot learn across sessions / verify every output instead of developing trust over time) to Osmani as if quoted; the source supports only part of it and does not state the cross-session-learning contrast. Clean synthesis hardened into an apparent source quote.
Finding 4's 'agents show no learning across sessions' is presented as a Goedecke claim but is the discovery agent's gloss; the article only implies independence-per-attempt.

Yamin's '90% inflated false positives' and 'personalized responsibility becomes mathematically unattainable' are authoritative-sounding numbers laundered through a Medium opinion post, with primary citations (arXiv:2601.14691; Romanchuk & Bondar 2026) that were not independently verified.

result.title: Operationalizing HITL gates in agentic engineering

discovery.summary: Leading 2026 agentic engineering workflows converge on a small vocabulary of human checkpoints and are shifting from human-IN-the-loop (approve every action) toward human-ON-the-loop (supervise and intervene when it matters). The dominant concrete gate placement is a three-gate pattern around the autonomous coding loop — Plan Review (before file touch), Findings/Exploration Review (after codebase investigation, before edits), and Diff-Before-Push (before commit/merge) — explicitly designed to avoid the approval-fatigue failure mode of gating every tool call. Spec-driven development (GitHub Spec Kit, open-sourced 2026-05-09) operationalizes the same idea as phase boundaries (Specify→Plan→Tasks→Implement) where humans approve the spec, plan, and tests before autonomous implementation is unlocked. The clearest ask-vs-act primary source is Anthropic's Claude Code auto mode (2026-03-25): a Sonnet-4.6 transcript classifier runs in-repo edits freely but gates shell/external/out-of-project actions against 20+ block rules, escalating to the human after 3 consecutive or 20 total denials; Anthropic's companion research reframes autonomy as a 1-10 emergent property and shows experienced users grant MORE autonomy yet intervene MORE. Enterprise framing (Digital Applied, Waxell) adds risk/reversibility-tiered gate taxonomies (Advisory/Validating/Blocking/Escalating; Free-run/Monitor-and-flag/Block-for-approval) enforced in a governance layer rather than agent code, mapped to NIST/ISO 42001/EU AI Act Art. 14. METR's rising time horizons (14.5h at 50%) are read as changing the nature of failure (rarer, more complex, harder to catch) rather than removing the need for well-placed gates.

key_findings.claim: The dominant concrete gate placement for autonomous coding agents is three strategic gates: Plan Review (before file touch), Findings Review (after exploration, before edits), and Diff-Before-Push (before commit), chosen as highest-leverage, lowest-cost intervention points.
key_findings.evidence: Three minimal approval gates—plan review, findings review, and diff-before-push—cover real agent risk... gates work when they surface moments that actually require human judgment — not when they interrupt every tool invocation. Sequence: Task → Plan Gate → Exploration → Findings Gate → Implementation → Diff Gate → Commit.
key_findings.source_title: Where to Gate Your AI Coding Agent: A 3-Checkpoint Framework

key_findings.claim: Claude Code auto mode replaces per-action approval with a Sonnet-4.6 transcript classifier that runs in-repo edits freely but gates shell/external/out-of-project actions against 20+ block rules, escalating to the human after 3 consecutive denials or 20 total denials per session.
key_findings.evidence: The system escalates when: Three consecutive denials occur... Twenty total denials accumulate across a session. Block-rule categories: destroy/exfiltrate, security-posture degradation, trust-boundary crossing, review-bypass. 'effective oversight doesn't require approving every action but being in a position to intervene when it matters.'
key_findings.source_title: How we built Claude Code auto mode: a safer way to skip permissions

key_findings.claim: Anthropic measures autonomy as an emergent 1-10 property (not fixed levels); experienced users grant MORE autonomy (>40% full auto-approval vs ~20% for new users) yet intervene MORE (9% vs 5%), shifting from per-action approval to monitoring-and-intervene.
key_findings.evidence: experienced users grant more autonomy while simultaneously intervening more frequently. Agent-initiated pauses: presenting alternatives (35%), gathering diagnostics (21%), requesting credentials (12%); Claude asks clarification 2x+ more on complex tasks; 73% involve humans somewhere in the loop.
key_findings.source_title: Measuring AI agent autonomy in practice

key_findings.claim: Spec-driven development (GitHub Spec Kit) operationalizes HITL as phase-boundary approval gates: humans validate the spec (no [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] markers), approve the plan, and per its constitution approve tests before any implementation code is generated.
key_findings.evidence: 'Tests are validated and approved by the user' before implementation begins; '[NEEDS CLARIFICATION: specific question]' markers must be resolved through human dialogue before progression. Humans decide requirements/acceptance/trade-offs; AI executes generation/code.
key_findings.source_title: spec-kit/spec-driven.md

key_findings.claim: Enterprise governance framing defines four risk/reversibility-keyed gate types — Advisory (log-only), Validating (async 4-24h SLA), Blocking (synchronous 15-min SLA), Escalating (conditional routing) — with the reviewer barred from being the workflow's Responsible party, mapped to NIST AI RMF / ISO 42001 / EU AI Act Art. 14.
key_findings.evidence: 'Reviewer must NOT be Responsible on the workflow.' Three SLA tiers: 15-min (real-time), 4-hour (standard), 24-hour (complex). Nine-field audit schema including 'state diff at gate.'
key_findings.source_title: Agentic Workflow Approval Gates: Governance Framework

key_findings.claim: 2026 designs shift toward human-on-the-loop (autonomous execution + monitoring + reactive intervention) for reversible/low-stakes work, reserving blocking HITL gates for irreversible/high-stakes/regulated actions; oversight policy should live in a governance layer, not agent code.
key_findings.evidence: Three-tier rubric — Tier 1 free-run (read-only/internal), Tier 2 monitor-and-flag (reversible external, HOTL), Tier 3 block-for-approval (irreversible/high-stakes, HITL). 'a tiered approval policy—enforced at the governance layer, not inside agent code—solves all three problems.'
key_findings.source_title: Human-in-the-Loop vs Human-on-the-Loop for AI Agents

key_findings.claim: ESCALATE.md is an open MIT file convention declaring when an agent must seek approval: TRIGGERS (prod deploys, external comms, financial txns, permanent deletion, privilege changes, cost-threshold), CHANNELS (per-channel timeouts), and APPROVAL (default 30-min timeout + fallback), with full audit logging.
key_findings.evidence: 'A plain-text file convention for defining human notification and approval protocols in AI agent projects.' Notifications include action, justification, estimated cost, reversibility, alternatives, session ID, deadline; approval via email reply, Slack reaction, or signed API POST.
key_findings.source_title: ESCALATE.md — The AI Agent Human Approval Protocol

key_findings.claim: Autonomous agent platforms place the human checkpoint at the pull-request boundary: the agent runs in a sandbox and returns a PR for review — the engineer re-enters at checkpoints, not at every keystroke (Devin; Goldman Sachs pilot, Nubank migrations).
key_findings.evidence: 'Devin... returns a pull request for human review. The engineer re-enters at checkpoints, not at every keystroke.' Reliability: 'an agent that succeeds on 90% of tasks but fails unpredictably on the remaining 10% may be a useful assistant yet an unacceptable autonomous system.'
key_findings.source_title: Devin AI Complete Guide: Autonomous Software Engineering

key_findings.claim: METR's rising autonomous task time horizon (14.5h at 50% for Claude Opus 4.6, ~7-month doubling) is read as changing the nature of failure — rarer but more complex and harder to catch — rather than removing the need for well-placed gates.
key_findings.evidence: 'Doubling the time horizon doesn't double the degree of automation — it changes the nature of failure. Errors become rarer but more complex and harder to catch. A silent reconciliation error building over three weeks is categorically different from a flagged exception on a single transaction.'
key_findings.source_title: Time Horizon 1.1 / Task-Completion Time Horizons of Frontier AI Models

key_findings.claim: Cross-source consensus anti-pattern: defaulting to 'interrupt on everything' destroys agent value and trains rubber-stamping, while 'interrupt on nothing' is unsafe; a 'high but not 100%' approval rate is the health signal (100% = rubber stamp).
key_findings.evidence: Anthropic: manual-approval mode leads users to 'accept 93% of prompts.' Grass: approval rates should be 'high but not 100%... Perfect approval signals gates have become rubber stamps.' Multiple: developers 'default to one extreme or the other.'
key_findings.source_title: How we built Claude Code auto mode + 3-Checkpoint Framework

named_frameworks: 3-Checkpoint Framework (Grass): Plan Review / Findings Review / Diff-Before-Push gates
Claude Code Auto Mode (Anthropic): prompt-injection probe + Sonnet-4.6 transcript classifier, 3 permission tiers, 20+ block rules, 3-consecutive/20-total denial escalation
Claude Code Permission Modes: default / Plan Mode / Auto Mode / --dangerously-skip-permissions
Autonomy 1-10 emergent scale (Anthropic 'Measuring agent autonomy')
Four-Gate-Type taxonomy (Digital Applied): Advisory / Validating / Blocking / Escalating with 15min/4h/24h SLA tiers + RACI separation
Three-Tier approval rubric (Waxell): Free-run / Monitor-and-flag (HOTL) / Block-for-approval (HITL)
Human-in-the-loop vs Human-on-the-loop (HITL vs HOTL) distinction
Spec-Driven Development phase gates (GitHub Spec Kit): Specify→Plan→Tasks→Implement, [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] markers, Phase -1 Gates, test-approval gate
ESCALATE.md (WellStrategic): TRIGGERS / CHANNELS / APPROVAL file convention
Change-set state machine (Kilo/StackAI): INTENT→SPEC→PLAN→IMPLEMENT→VERIFY→DOCS→REVIEW→RELEASE→MONITOR→ITERATE
METR HCAST / Time-Horizon (TH1.1): 50%-task-completion time horizon as an autonomy measure
debates_tensions: HITL vs HOTL direction of travel: practitioner sources report a shift toward human-on-the-loop autonomy, while governance/enterprise sources keep blocking HITL gates mandatory for irreversible/regulated actions — reconciled by tiering on risk/reversibility.

Is HITL real oversight? An April 2026 MIT Technology Review piece (cited secondhand) argues 'humans in the loop' has become an illusion because overseers cannot verify internal model reasoning, cutting against spec-driven phase-gate optimism.

Where policy lives: Waxell insists oversight rules belong in a governance layer (not agent code, which drifts), while Anthropic bakes much policy into a model classifier and ESCALATE.md puts it in a repo file — three different loci for the same rules.

Does longer time horizon reduce the need for gates? METR's hours-long autonomy could argue for fewer interruptions, but the practitioner reading is failures become rarer and harder to catch, arguing for better-placed gates rather than fewer.

Approval-rate calibration target: Anthropic observes 93% blind approval under manual mode (fatigue evidence); Grass prescribes 'high but not 100%' — no agreed numeric target, only that 100% = rubber-stamp and frequent rejection = upstream workflow problem.
discovery.recency_note: Evidence is strongly current: the strongest sources are all 2026 (Anthropic auto mode 2026-03-25, Anthropic agent-autonomy research 2026-02-18, METR TH1.1 2026-01-29, GitHub Spec Kit open-sourced 2026-05-09, Grass 3-checkpoint 2026-05-03, Digital Applied and Waxell both 2026-04-27). This is a fast-moving area — Claude Code permission modes, auto mode, and Spec Kit have all shipped or changed within the last ~4 months, and the HITL→HOTL framing is actively being debated in real time, so specifics (denial thresholds, classifier model versions, time-horizon numbers) should be re-verified against vendor docs before being treated as durable.

verdicts.claim: Claude Code auto mode uses a Sonnet 4.6 transcript classifier, runs in-repo edits freely but gates shell/external/out-of-project actions against 20+ block rules, escalating after 3 consecutive or 20 total denials per session.
verdicts.note: Source confirms Sonnet 4.6 classifier, 'more than twenty block rules' in four groups (destroy/exfiltrate, degrade security, cross trust boundaries, bypass review), 3-consecutive/20-total escalation, FPR 8.5%->0.4%, and the 93% approval figure. BUT the evidence string attaches the quote 'effective oversight doesn't require approving every action but being in a position to intervene when it matters' to THIS page; that quote is not on the auto-mode page (it is from Anthropic's Measuring Agent Autonomy page). Misattributed quote within an otherwise-confirmed claim.

verdicts.claim: Anthropic measures autonomy as an emergent (not fixed-level) property; experienced users grant more autonomy (>40% full auto-approval vs ~20%) yet intervene more (9% vs 5%); pauses 35/21/12%; clarification 2x+ on complex tasks; 73% have a human in the loop.
verdicts.note: All numeric specifics verified verbatim in source: '~20%' vs '>40%' auto-approval, '5%' vs 'around 9%' interrupt, present-choice 35% / diagnostics 21% / credentials 12%, 'more than twice as often' clarification, '73% appear to have a human in the loop in some way', and 'not a fixed property... emergent characteristic'. Minor: the literal '1-10 scale' is a paraphrase; source says 'emergent characteristic'.

verdicts.claim: The dominant/canonical concrete gate placement for autonomous coding agents is three strategic gates (Plan Review, Findings Review, Diff-Before-Push), a cross-source consensus pattern.
verdicts.note: The blog fully supports the three gates, the Task->Plan->Exploration->Findings->Implementation->Diff->Commit sequence, and the 'judgment not every tool invocation' quote. But independent search for the three gate names returns ONLY codeongrass.com (Grass) pages. This is one vendor blog's coined framework, not an independently corroborated 'dominant/canonical/consensus' industry pattern. The 'dominant pattern' elevation is unsupported.

verdicts.claim: GitHub Spec Kit (open-sourced 2026-05-09) operationalizes HITL as phase-boundary gates: validate spec (no [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] markers), approve plan (Phase -1 gates), approve tests before implementation.
verdicts.note: Source confirms 'Tests are validated and approved by the user' before implementation, [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] markers, Specify->Plan->Tasks->Implement, and 'Phase -1: Pre-Implementation Gates'. 2026-05-09 open-source date corroborated by GitHub Blog, MarkTechPost, WinBuzzer, Visual Studio Magazine independently.

verdicts.claim: METR's rising autonomous task time horizon is 14.5h at 50% for Claude Opus 4.6 (~7-month doubling), read as 'changing the nature of failure' rather than removing gates.
verdicts.note: The cited Jan-29-2026 page reports Claude Opus 4.5 at ~4h49m (~5.3h), NOT 14.5h and NOT Opus 4.6. The 14.5h/Opus-4.6 figure is from a LATER measurement reported ~2026-02-21 (e.g. officechai), wrongly pinned to the Jan-29 URL. The 'doubling... changes the nature of failure / silent reconciliation error over three weeks' quote does NOT appear on the METR page; it is secondary blog commentary, not METR. Source-type also inflated to 'peer-reviewed' (it is a research-org blog).

verdicts.claim: Enterprise governance framing defines four gate types (Advisory/Validating/Blocking/Escalating) with 15min/4h/24h SLAs, reviewer-not-Responsible rule, nine-field audit schema, mapped to NIST AI RMF / ISO 42001 / EU AI Act Art. 14.
verdicts.note: Every specific verified in source, including the 'Reviewer must NOT be Responsible' rule, the SLA tiers, the nine-field schema with 'agent state diff', and the compliance mapping. Provenance caveat: this is a single vendor's proprietary thought-leadership framework, not a standards-body artifact; the SLA numbers and schema are this blog's design proposal. Confidence 'medium' was appropriate.

verdicts.claim: ESCALATE.md is an open MIT file convention for declaring agent approval protocols (TRIGGERS / CHANNELS / APPROVAL, default 30-min timeout, audit logging).
verdicts.note: Spec content confirmed: MIT license, TRIGGERS (prod deploy, external comms, financial, permanent deletion, privilege, cost threshold), CHANNELS (email/Slack/PagerDuty), approval via email/Slack emoji/signed POST, 30-min default (escalating to KILLSWITCH). But the site self-describes as a conceptual/marketing-stage spec, the domain is listed for acquisition, and there is no evidence of real-world adoption. Framing as an established 'open standard' oversells it.

verdicts.claim: Autonomous agent platforms place the human checkpoint at the PR boundary; the engineer re-enters at checkpoints not every keystroke (Devin; Goldman Sachs pilot; Nubank migrations).
verdicts.note: Cited blog is low quality, but substance independently corroborated: Devin proposes a PR for human review (Cognition + multiple 2026 outlets), Goldman Sachs piloted Devin (first reported July 2025), Nubank ran large-scale migrations with ~12x efficiency. The 're-enters at checkpoints not every keystroke' line is the blog's paraphrase, not a Cognition primary quote. Better cited to Cognition's own posts.

contradicting_evidence: MIT Technology Review, 'Why having humans in the loop in an AI war is an illusion' (2026-04-16, https://www.technologyreview.com/2026/04/16/1136029/humans-in-the-loop-ai-war-illusion/) is a REAL first-party piece (the thread hedged it as 'cited secondhand'). It argues HITL becomes theatre when overseers cannot audit internal model reasoning, operate under workflow pressure, and suffer skill atrophy — directly qualifying the spec-driven phase-gate optimism and the broader 'well-placed gates solve oversight' thesis. Corroborated by Defense News (2026-03-26) and the Institute for Systems Integrity 'Human-with-Agency' piece.

The METR Jan-29-2026 page actually reports Claude Opus 4.5 at ~4h49m (~5.3h), not 14.5h. The 14.5h/Opus-4.6 number is from a separate, later (~Feb-21-2026) evaluation. So the thread's own recency caveat ('re-verify the time-horizon numbers') is well-founded — and the number was already mis-cited to the wrong page/model/date.

Independent search for the 'Plan Review / Findings Review / Diff-before-push' three-gate names surfaces only codeongrass.com (Grass) — there is no cross-vendor corroboration that this specific three-gate framework is an industry-dominant pattern, contradicting the synthesis's 'dominant/canonical/consensus' framing.
training_data_smells: 'The dominant concrete gate placement is a three-gate pattern' / 'canonical 3-gate' / 'cross-source consensus anti-pattern' — promotes a single vendor blog's (Grass) coined framework to industry consensus with no independent corroboration. The gate content is sound; the consensus framing is ungrounded.

METR 'Doubling the time horizon doesn't double the degree of automation — it changes the nature of failure... A silent reconciliation error building over three weeks...' — a vivid, quotable passage attributed to a 'peer-reviewed' METR source where it does not appear; it is secondary blog commentary (the raw file half-admits 'Apiar/METR commentary'). Classic too-clean-quote-attached-to-wrong-source.
ESCALATE.md framed as an established 'open standard / open convention' when the source is a marketing-stage spec with the domain listed for acquisition and no demonstrated adoption.
